Specifically, we construct a dual-branch encoder that combines a CNN backbone and frozen DINOv3 features via gated pyramid fusion, enabling rich multi-scale semantic representation. Then, a multi-scale temporal bidirectional transformer interaction (M-TBTT) module is proposed to achieve global cross-temporal feature alignment and information interaction. To further enhance genuine changes and suppress pseudo-variations, we introduce semantic purification (SCP), bidirectional change enhancement (BiChangeEnhance), and multi-scale change enhancement (MCE) modules collaboratively. Finally, a multi-branch CD prediction head is designed to jointly output binary change mask, bi-temporal semantic maps, and edge constraint. Extensive experiments on public remote sensing CD datasets demonstrate that SemDINO achieves superior performance and generalization ability against state-of-the-art methods, especially in complex scenarios with interference factors.

title: Difference-Aware Retrieval Policies for Imitation Learning
authors: Quinn Pfeifer, Ethan Pronovost, Paarth Shah, Khimya Khetarpal, Siddhartha Srinivasa, Abhishek Gupta
abstract: Parametric imitation learning via behavior cloning can suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution states due to compounding errors during deployment. We show that reusing the training data during inference via a semi-parametric retrieval-based imitation learning approach can alleviate this challenge. We present Difference-Aware Retrieval Policies for Imitation Learning (DARP), a semi-parametric retrieval-based imitation learning approach that addresses this limitation by reparameterizing the imitation learning problem in terms of local neighborhood structure rather than direct state-to-action mappings. Instead of learning a global policy, DARP trains a model to predict actions based on $k$-nearest neighbors from expert demonstrations, their corresponding actions, and the relative distance vectors between neighbor states and query states. DARP requires no additional assumptions beyond those made for standard behavior cloning -- it does not require additional data collection, online expert feedback, or task-specific knowledge. We demonstrate consistent performance improvements of 15-46% over standard behavior cloning across diverse domains, including continuous control and robotic manipulation, and across different representations, including high-dimensional visual features. Code and demos are available at https://weirdlabuw.github.io/darp-site/.

title: Partial Pressure Contributions of Hadron Families to the QCD Equation of State
authors: Jonathan Gonzales, Alejandro Florez, Johannes Jahan, Angel R. Nava Acuna, Naman Mehndiratta, Claudia Ratti
abstract: Lattice simulations provide the thermodynamics of quantum chromodynamics (QCD) as a function of the temperature, at zero-to-moderate values of the baryonic chemical potential. However, the contribution of single hadronic species cannot be directly isolated from lattice calculations. In this work, we find linear combinations of up to fourth order susceptibilities which isolate the contribution of hadrons to the QCD pressure according to their baryon number $B$, electric charge $Q$ and strangeness $S$ content. These combinations are valid, provided that the thermodynamics of a strongly-interacting gas in the low-temperature regime can be modeled as a gas of non-interacting hadrons and their resonances. Finally, we test the validity of these linear combinations in the Hadron Resonance Gas (HRG) model and compare them to available lattice QCD results, using continuum-estimated susceptibilities.

title: Sambe Approach to Floquet-Lindblad Open Quantum Systems
authors: Andriani Keliri, Marco Schirò
abstract: We study driven and open quantum systems described by a time-periodic Lindblad master equation. In closed systems, the stroboscopic dynamics can always be described by an effective time-independent Floquet Hamiltonian; this idea is the basis of Floquet engineering. However, in the presence of dissipation, the existence of an effective time-independent Floquet Lindbladian is not guaranteed due to the non-unitary nature of the evolution. Using Floquet theory, we construct a well-defined time-independent Floquet Lindbladian in an extended Sambe-Liouville space, transforming the initial time-dependent problem to a static and non-Hermitian eigenvalue problem. For harmonic driving, we introduce a matrix continued fraction method to nonperturbatively resum multiphoton processes and construct an effective Floquet Lindbladian acting only on the physical Liouville space. Compared to other high-frequency expansions, this method has the advantage of providing the whole infinite series expansion at once. Using a resolvent formalism, we show how to obtain a spectral Floquet representation of correlation functions of an open quantum system. As an application, we consider a dissipating two-level system in a linearly polarized field and calculate its resonance fluorescence spectrum. Furthermore, we consider a parametrically driven quantum dot with pump and loss for which we calculate its spectral function and current-voltage characteristics.

title: NEXUS: Abundance, Environments, and Spectral Diversity of Little Red Dots from the NIRSpec MSA Sample
authors: Zhiwei Pan, Ming-Yang Zhuang, Yue Shen, Feige Wang, Jenny E. Greene, Adam J. Burgasser, Junyao Li, Zachary Stone, Padmavathi Venkatraman
abstract: We present a comprehensive study of Little Red Dots (LRDs) at 2.3 < z < 7.4 using NIRCam photometry and NIRSpec MSA/PRISM spectra from the ongoing NEXUS program. Photometric selection combining several commonly adopted methods yields a high completeness of about 85% for LRD selection over this redshift range and for a flux limit of F444W < 26. The overall purity is about 60%, with contamination from emission-line galaxies and normal active galactic nuclei (AGNs), as well as dwarf stars. Most (>90%) of the spectroscopically confirmed LRDs have robust broad-line detection. Our spectroscopic sample of 36 LRDs displays the full range of spectral diversity of LRDs. It includes objects with extreme Balmer breaks similar to the LRD "Cliff", as well as objects with moderately reddened rest-optical continua that can be fit with low-temperature blackbody components in the recent BH* model framework. The broad H$α$ emission is correlated with the continuum emission at 5100 Angstrom, suggesting common origins for these emission components; the narrow [O III] emission, however, is poorly correlated with the optical continuum. We do not find evidence of redshift evolution in these spectral properties. The space density of LRDs declines toward z about 2, opposite to the trend for normal AGNs, although low-luminosity LRDs at z about 2-4 may be more abundant than currently probed by ground-based searches. The clustering of LRDs suggests that they live in dark matter halos of several times $10^{11}\ h^{-1}$ solar masses, albeit with large uncertainties. Overall, these results are consistent with recent observations of LRDs and with the emerging picture of accreting SMBHs enshrouded in dense gas envelopes as the origin of LRDs.

title: Elastoinertial effects govern dynamic response of soft hair beds
authors: Jonas Smucker, Naiesa Freeman, Eric Caballero, Philip J. Morrison, José Alvarado
abstract: Fluid-immersed hair beds are ubiquitous in biology-from the endothelial glycocalyx and primary cilia to intestinal microvilli-where they serve as mechanosensors that transduce dynamic flow signals into biochemical regulatory responses. Despite the inherently dynamic nature of physiological flows, the dynamic mechanical properties of fluid-immersed hair beds under time-varying conditions remain poorly characterized. Here we investigate the transient rheological response of elastic hair beds to large-amplitude oscillatory shear flows at low to intermediate Reynolds number. While the hairs and fluid themselves obey linear constitutive laws, their coupled interaction produces a dynamic nonlinear response that depends sensitively on driving frequency and amplitude. We identify a crossover from a stress-lagging regime to a stress-leading regime, which is governed by an interplay between fluid viscosity, fluid inertia, and hair elasticity. A simplified rigid-beam model qualitatively captures the crossover behavior. Characterizing the dynamic flow response of soft hair beds has direct biological implications, since the lag time sensitively determines the stability of mechanosensory signaling in the feedback loops underlying essential biological processes such as vasodilation, ciliary remodeling, and tubular reabsorption. Our results establish a framework for understanding how the physical properties of biological hair beds optimize dynamic information transmission during mechanotransduction.

title: Degenerate Diffusions on Continuum Percolation and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Equations
authors: Rodrigo Bazaes, Alexander Mielke, Chiranjib Mukherjee
abstract: We study degenerate controlled diffusions and Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equations posed on genuine continuum percolation clusters. The diffusion is constrained to evolve inside the infinite cluster and degenerates according to the distance to the irregular random boundary. Under suitable regularity and degeneracy assumptions on the diffusion matrix, we prove that the corresponding controlled diffusion never reaches the boundary and therefore admits a unique global strong solution. Using this result, we establish a stochastic control representation formula for viscosity solutions of degenerate Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equations posed on the random cluster, without imposing boundary conditions. We further verify that the structural assumptions introduced in this work, including quantitative integrability properties of the distance-to-the-boundary function and the associated degeneracy, hold for concrete continuum percolation models such as the Boolean model. In particular, although the diffusion never reaches the boundary, the boundary geometry remains a fundamental ingredient of the theory through the admissible degeneracy regime. The results establish a quenched framework for stochastic control and degenerate partial differential equations on genuine continuum percolation geometries, and identify a link between the analytic structure of the diffusion and the stochastic geometry of the underlying cluster. They also provide the foundational framework for the homogenization theory developed in the companion article [BMM26].

title: Codifferential Calculi on Quantum Homogeneous Spaces
authors: Julius Benner
abstract: We develop the theory of first- and higher-order codifferential calculi over coalgebras $C$ over fields $k$ with characteristic $\mathrm{char}(k)\neq 2$. For a given first-order codifferential calculus, we introduce its maximal prolongation by means of an explicit construction that associates to it a differential graded coalgebra, satisfying a universal property. For module coalgebras over a Hopf algebra $U$, we introduce the notion of an equivariant codifferential calculus. If $C$ is of the form $U\otimes_H k$ for a Hopf algebra $U$ and a right coideal subalgebra $H$ such that $U$ is faithfully flat as a left- and right $H$-module, we show that equivariant first-order codifferential calculi correspond to certain right coideals $T\subseteq \ker(\varepsilon\colon C\rightarrow k)$ called quantum tangent spaces. If $H$ is a sub bialgebra and the right $C$-coaction on $T$ is trivial, then the maximal prolongation is described in terms of a quadratic coalgebra. We further relate codifferential calculi to differential calculi and Cartan pairs over the dual algebra $C^\ast$, or more generally subalgebras thereof. We explicitly compute codifferential calculi on the coalgebra pre duals of the Podleś sphere and the quantized projective spaces. As an application, we give a new proof that the antiholomorphic Heckenberger--Kolb calculi on quantized projective spaces have classical dimension.

title: Exact Four-Parameter Rotating NS--NS Vacuum in Double Field Theory
authors: Hun Jang, Minkyoo Kim, Jeong-Hyuck Park
abstract: We construct an exact rotating vacuum of the NS--NS sector, equivalently a Double Field Theory vacuum. The construction applies compact $\mathbf{SO}(2)$ S-duality to a rotating Einstein--scalar seed. The solution has four independent parameters $\{\mathfrak{m},j,\mathfrak{q},ζ\}$. To our knowledge, this is the first explicit rotating solution of the pure NS--NS vacuum equations with all three NS--NS fields $\{g,B,φ\}$ determined analytically, independent dilaton and $H$-flux charges, and no Maxwell sector. The static limit is obtained by taking the rotation parameter $j\to 0$. In this limit the geometry is not the spherical Burgess--Myers--Quevedo solution. Instead, it is an axial Zipoy--Voorhees branch carrying $H$-flux, so an oblate deformation remains after rotation is switched off. This geometric memory is absent in pure general relativity and in Einstein--Maxwell--dilaton--axion. The two static branches nevertheless share the same $\ell=0$ parametrized post-Newtonian data $\{MG,β_{\mathrm{PPN}},γ_{\mathrm{PPN}},h\}$. They give two inequivalent NS--NS geometries at identical monopole charges, with the degeneracy lifted at $\ell=2$. At the Kerr horizon locus the outer shell is generically singular in curvature. Above the threshold $|\mathfrak{q}|>\sqrt{\mathfrak{m}^{2}-j^{2}}$, polar geodesics are repelled outward and the rotation axis becomes regular in curvature at the shell. On that axis the inverse metric $g^{μν}$ stays finite while the lower-index Riemannian metric components diverge, whereas off the axis the inverse metric itself diverges. This axis-local degeneracy may offer a setting for non-Riemannian geometry in Double Field Theory, where $g_{μν}$ is not fundamental and the $\mathbf{O}(D,D)$ variables $\{d,\mathcal{H}_{AB}\}$ remain well defined.

title: Online change point detection under heavy-tailedness and contamination
authors: Edwin Yiu Nam Tang, Yudong Chen, Mengchu Li, Yi Yu
abstract: We study an online version of the robust mean change point detection problem under a dynamic Huber contamination model with arbitrary contamination distribution and inlier distribution possessing exponentially- or polynomially-decaying tails. This robustness framework is systematically studied for the first time in the change point literature. For univariate data, we characterise the detection delay by partitioning the parameter space into four regimes, in terms of the true change location, signal size and contamination level. Efficient detection procedures are accompanied by matching lower bounds, up to poly-logarithmic factors. For the multivariate setting, we devise an efficient robust mean testing procedure and apply this to the robust online change point problem. The theoretical analysis of the robust mean testing procedure is the first in dealing with both Huber contamination and heavy-tailedness, and is thus of independent interest. Extensive numerical experiments are conducted to support our theoretical findings.

title: A space-time sparse-grid method for the wave equation
authors: Matteo Ferrari, Andrea Moiola, Chiara Perinati, Ilaria Perugia
abstract: We develop a fast space-time numerical scheme for approximating solutions to the linear wave equation. The approach is based on the sparse-grid combination technique applied to a coercive space-time discretization. Designed for tensor-product space-time discretizations, the method enables efficient parallelization of the resulting solver. We provide a rigorous theoretical analysis establishing convergence rates and computational complexity estimates. Numerical experiments validate the theoretical estimates and demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed method.

title: Conceptualising Reflective Use: Toward A Process Perspective On Human-AI Interaction
authors: Thimo Schulz, Christina Speck
abstract: The rapid diffusion of generative artificial intelligence (genAI) systems reshapes how individuals engage with information systems, requiring users to monitor, assess, and adapt their interaction with non-deterministic systems. Existing constructs capture elements of this engagement but do not account for the situated dynamics of the entire evaluative process in genAI use. This research-in-progress, situated in a larger endeavour towards a scale development, derives an initial conceptualisation of reflective use: a behavioural-knowledge capability that unfolds across pre-use, in-use, and post-use phases, reinforced through situated reflective knowledge gained in practice. Drawing on expert interviews and a focus group, we identify four core components of reflective use and show how they form an iterative capability cycle anchored within the motivational needs outlined in self-determination theory. Understanding reflective use is essential to ensure appropriate reliance and high decision quality, and thus provides a foundation for promoting responsible and effective human-AI interaction.

title: Mean field games with option to buy information
authors: Bernardo D'Auria, Markus Fischer
abstract: We introduce a class of continuous time finite horizon mean field games where the objective function of the representative player depends on a hidden state, in addition to position, control, and the population distribution. While acting on the position dynamics, the agent has the option to pay for seeing the hidden state. We connect the original formulation of our model with a mean field model of optimal control with discretionary stopping, characterize solutions, and give a simple explicitly solvable example. For a class of $N$-player games with compatible information structure, we show that approximate Nash equilibria can be constructed starting from a solution to the limit model.

title: SEF-CLGC at SemEval-2026 Task 11: Logical Notation Impact on Language Model Performance
authors: Hanna Abi Akl, Fabien Gandon, Catherine Faron, Pierre Monnin
abstract: This paper revisits our pipeline called Syllogistic Evaluation Framework-Common Logic Grammar Construction (SEF-CLGC). We combine formal logical notations with Small Language Models (SLMs) to evaluate reasoning performance on the SemEval-2026 Task 11 Subtask 1: Disentangling Content and Formal Reasoning in Large Language Models. Our experiments show that by relying solely on SLMs, trained on a combination of natural and symbolic languages, our best model achieves a content score of 27.80% on the task while significantly lowering the content bias in reasoning.

title: Finite-$t$ and target mass corrections for the short-distance expansion of quasi(pseudo) GPDs
authors: Vladimir M. Braun, Hua-Yu Jiang
abstract: We calculate the ``kinematic'' corrections $t/P_z^2$ and $m_N^2/P_z^2$ to the short distance expansion of gauge-invariant nonlocal quark-antiquark operators sandwiched between nucleon states with different momenta. Here $t$ is the momentum transfer, $m_N$ is the nucleon mass and $P_z$ is the momentum component in the direction of the quark-antiquark separation, which is assumed to be large. These matrix elements can be calculated in lattice QCD and, at leading twist, expressed in terms of moments of the generalized parton distrubutions (GPDs). Our results allow one to control one of principal uncertainties in such calculations and extend their region of applicability to larger momentum transfers, which is important in the quest to access the three-dimension image of the proton. The calculated corrections turn out to be significant for a realistic lattice QCD setup.

title: The $L^p$ Neumann problem for parabolic operators with coefficients satisfying small Carleson condition
authors: Martin Dindoš, Linhan Li, Jill Pipher
abstract: In this paper, we resolve the question of whether the Neumann problem for the parabolic PDE $-\partial_tu + \mathrm{div}(A\nabla u)=0$ on a Lipschitz cylinder $\mathcal O\times\mathbb R$ is solvable for some $p\in (1,\infty)$ under the assumption that the matrix $A$ is elliptic with bounded and measurable coefficients that satisfy a natural Carleson condition (a parabolic analog of the so-called DKP-condition). We prove that for any $1<p<\infty$ the Neumann problem is solvable under the assumption that both the Carleson norm of coefficients and the Lipschitz constant of the domain are sufficiently small (with dependence on $p$). The question of what happens in the "large Carleson norm/large Lipschitz constant" regime remains open, and even for elliptic PDEs this question has only been resolved in two dimensions. This paper complements results from our recent manuscript (by the same authors) in which the parabolic regularity problem has been fully resolved in both the small and large Carleson norm regime. Previously, the Dirichlet problem had been resolved under the same conditions by various authors.

title: High-degree vertices in uniform recursive directed acyclic graphs with freezing
authors: Rafael Engel, Bas Lodewijks
abstract: We study uniform recursive directed acyclic graphs with freezing. Here, a graph is built by adding vertices one-by-one and connecting a new vertex to $m\in\mathbb N$ uniformly selected vertices already present. At certain steps vertices can also be frozen, and arriving vertices are not allowed to connect to frozen vertices. This model generalises the uniform attachment tree with freezing, introduced by Bellin et. al (which corresponds to the case $m=1$) as well as the uniform recursive directed acyclic graph model (where no vertices are frozen). Under mild assumptions on when vertices are frozen, we study the empirical degree distribution, large degrees in the graph, and other properties of large-degree vertices such as their label and distance to the first vertex in the graph. Our work improves and/or extends various results from the literature on uniform attachment trees (with freezing) and uniform recursive directed acyclic graphs without freezing. In particular, our results show that statistics that are determined `locally' (e.g. the empirical degree distribution and maximum degree) are essentially unaffected by the freezing of vertices, whereas statistics that are determined `globally' (e.g. the length of paths between vertices) are highly affected by introducing freezing. The analysis relies on adapting the Kingman coalescent construction for uniform attachment trees to the non-tree setting.

title: Control transition in a temporally random classical spin chain
authors: Elisha Shmalo, J. H. Pixley, Manas Kulkarni, Sarang Gopalakrishnan, David A. Huse
abstract: We theoretically explore a phase transition between controlled and chaotic dynamics in a classical spin chain model subject to chaotic Hamiltonian dynamics and a contractive "control map", which alternate in time. The control map drives the system toward a target configuration that is an unstable fixed point under the chaotic dynamics. When the control is strong enough, the target configuration is the globally attracting stable fixed point of the dynamics; for weaker control, the many-body dynamics remains chaotic for almost all initial states. The phase transition between controlled and chaotic phases has a mixed character: As the transition is approached from the chaotic phase, the fraction of the spins that are far from the target configuration goes continuously to zero, and there are diverging spatial and temporal correlation lengths; however, the leading Lyapunov exponent is discontinuous across the transition, jumping from a positive value in the chaotic phase to a negative value in the controlled phase. We present evidence that this transition is in the same universality class as directed percolation in the presence of temporal randomness, a universality class for which we obtain results that are consistent with the dynamical Harris criterion but do not saturate the bound.

title: Bayesian analysis of the shear modulus in the neutron-star crust
authors: T. Diverrès, G. Montefusco, A. F. Fantina, F. Gulminelli
abstract: The elastic properties of the neutron-star crust are important for the calculations of crustal modes. In particular, the ability of the crust to support shear stresses has been connected to observations of quasi-periodic oscillations and to crust deformations potentially emitting gravitational waves. In this work, we assess the uncertainties in the shear modulus and shear speed in the neutron-star outer and inner crust. To this aim, we performed a Bayesian analysis of the shear properties of the neutron-star crust at zero temperature starting from both a non-informative and a nuclear-physics-informed prior. For the treatment of inhomogeneous matter in the crust, we relied on the one-component plasma approximation, with a (semi-)classical treatment of the ions. We show that the use of a nuclear-physics-informed prior has a non-negligible impact on the prediction of the elastic properties of the crust. The frequency of the fundamental torsional crustal modes we obtain is compatible with the low-frequency range of observed quasi-periodic oscillations, our estimates lying in the interval $\approx 20 - 50$~Hz. Although the different considered priors lead to compatible results, the inclusion of nuclear-physics experimental information in the prior considerably reduces the uncertainties in the prediction of the elastic properties of the crust, potentially constraining the predicted frequency of the crustal modes.

title: CityPulse 360 – AI-Powered Real-Time Urban Happiness and Sentiment Analysis
authors: Sai Charan Naidu P, J Naveen Krishna, A Akash, J Vijayarani
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The rapidly growing urbanization in India has introduced a demand of intelligent tools that will be used to monitor the well-being of the city in real time. Traditional urban performance indicators utilize stagnant or lagging data and can be unable to reflect the present public feeling. CityPulse 360 brings in an AI-based system merging social media mood, environmental factors, price-of-living information, and infrastructure indicators and produce a real-time mood of happiness in Indian cities. The system uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Machine Learning (ML) to process live streams in the format of weather and air-quality data provided by OpenWeatherMap API, sentiment-based data provided by Twitter, Reddit, and YouTube, and structured data in the form of safety, healthcare, affordability. These multimodal inputs are aggregated into composite scores and represented with the help of interactive Power BI dashboard. In this paper, the architecture of the system, data acquisition, preprocessing, feature engineering, and dashboard design will be presented to monitor the urban happiness. The framework provides assistance to policy makers, researchers and citizens by providing an ever-present and multi-dimensional picture of urban well-being.

title: Deepfake Video Forensics Using ResNeXt–LSTM Visual Modeling and DSP-Enhanced Audio Spectrogram Analysis
authors: Tanish Lal O, Sijo Devassy V J, P. Deepa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: The technology for creating deepfakes has advanced rapidly, and this allows for realistic modifications to both audio and video. Many current forensic systems only use frame-based visual artefacts, while modern deepfake pipelines use temporal smoothing, high-quality generative models, and synthetic voice, which makes unimodal detectors less dependable. This research introduces a multimodal deepfake detection framework that integrates a ResNeXt50 video feature extractor, an Long Short Term Memory (LSTM)-based temporal analysis module, and a Digital Signal Processing (DSP)-enhanced audio spectrogram classifier. Visual characteristics find differences in faces, temporal modelling finds differences between frames, and the audio stream finds problems in synthetic speech, like odd harmonic patterns and smoothed spectrum energy. A fusion module takes predictions from all branches and merges them to give a single authenticity score. Experiments show that using both DSP-based audio cues and temporal modelling makes things far more stable than only using video. The system is set up using a Django web interface that is built for automatic real-time deepfake verification.

title: A Dual-Level Data-Driven Framework for Reducing Financial Fraud in Banking
authors: Nusrat Jahan, Sheikh Razia Sultana, Anika Sayeema, Sabiha Firdaus, Jannatul Maua, Hashibul Ahsan Shoaib
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Financial fraud detection remains a major challenge in modern banking due to evolving adversarial manipulation, data imbalance, and the narrow focus of conventional detection strategies. This paper introduces a dual-level data-driven framework that combines account-level behavioral risk modeling with transaction-level anomaly detection to enable more accurate and context-aware fraud identification. The proposed system achieves an ROC-AUC of 0.995 and AUPRC of 0.907 under the final combined inference model, surpassing traditional classifiers such as Logistic Regression (AUPRC 0.716) and even deep MLP models (AUPRC 0.843). Under stringent operating requirements of 90% precision, the proposed model maintains a recall of 0.611 compared to 0.519 for deep MLP and 0.381 for Logistic Regression. Additionally, the fairness-aware account model reduces demographic prediction disparity from 0.178 to 0.081. These results, validated on both the Credit Card Fraud dataset and the BAF account dataset, demonstrate that multi-scale risk reasoning substantially improves operational fraud detection performance and provides practical benefit for real-world banking applications and fraud monitoring workflows.

title: Performance Comparison of Radiative Transfer Simulation Tools Based on Land Surface Radiance in Beihang Green Garden
authors: Yunda Yang, Huaiwen Yang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: To evaluate the performance of radiation transfer simulation tools in ground object radiance modeling, this study conducted research at Beijing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics' Lvyuan campus, selecting typical ground objects including vegetation, water bodies, soil, and buildings. Radiance data were extracted from Gaofen-1 satellite imagery and ASTGM TM V003 elevation data, with simulation scenarios constructed using three projection methods: fisheye camera, parallel projection camera, and perspective camera. Three mainstream radiation transfer simulation tools—LESS, DART, and ERADIATE—were employed for radiance modeling. A quantitative evaluation system was established, including absolute radiance error, relative error, root mean square error (RMSE), and coefficient of determination, combined with qualitative dimensions such as morphological fidelity and scene adaptability to systematically compare simulation accuracy under various solar geometric conditions. Results demonstrated that the LESS model consistently outperformed others across all observation conditions, maintaining a stable coefficient of determination above 0.99 and achieving the lowest RMSE across all experimental groups, showcasing superior numerical stability and morphological fidelity. Leveraging ray tracing algorithms, LESS demonstrated significant advantages in complex 3D scene simulations (vegetation-water-body-building mixtures), particularly excelling in fine-grained vegetation canopy structure representation, making it an ideal tool for scientific quantitative remote sensing inversion. The DART model exhibited balanced performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> generally above 0.95 and RMSE slightly higher than LESS but still within acceptable ranges. DART demonstrates robust performance in multiscale simulations (from individual blades to landscape scales) and multi-angle radiative transfer analysis, particularly exhibiting strong adaptability when handling heterogeneous surfaces, making it suitable for engineering simulation validation and parameter inversion applications. The ERADIATE model shows significant uncertainty in complex projection conditions, with RMSE values notably higher than the other two models. While its high-precision radiative transfer algorithm performs well in idealized simple scenarios, simulation accuracy and stability are relatively limited in highly heterogeneous urban environments like Beihang Green Park due to constraints in scene modeling flexibility and computational resource availability. However, ERADIATE demonstrates lower coefficient of variation under weak signal conditions (e.g., cloudy days or water bodies), indicating its advantages under low-energy input scenarios. This study provides quantitative evidence for selecting and optimizing radiation transfer simulation tools in complex surface environments, while establishing methodological foundations for precision evaluation and parameter calibration of simulation tools in quantitative remote sensing applications.

title: Dynamic Data-Driven Framework for Forecasting Employment Trends in the AI Era
authors: Mayank Singh Negi, Vansh Raheja, Ketan Kumar, Meena Chaudhary, Gunjan Chandwani, Narender Gautam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping global labor markets by automating repetitive tasks while creating new opportunities in data-driven and technology-intensive sectors. This paper presents a Dynamic Data-Driven Employment Forecasting Model (DDEFM) that predicts employment trends using a hybrid machine learning framework integrating time-series forecasting, natural language processing, and sectoral analysis. The model combines real-world labor datasets, AI adoption metrics, and investment indicators to evaluate employment volatility across sectors. The research contributes a technical methodology to forecast job creation and decline across industries such as healthcare, IT, education, and green energy. Experimental results demonstrate the model’s ability to reduce prediction error by 12–18% compared to baseline statistical models. This study provides a scalable framework for policymakers and industries to plan workforce development in the AI era.

title: Deep Learning-Based Intelligent Intrusion Detection System For The Internet Of Medical Things
authors: B. Rama Subba Reddy, Munagala Kotthakapa Sarath Kumar Reddy, Ponnaganti Baba Ganesh, Raghavarapu Naga Sai Rusheek, Soleti Venkatasatyakarthik
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: The project mentioned as the HIDS-IoMT A Deep Learning-Based Intelligent Intrusion Detection System of the Internet of medical Things explains a safe and intelligent approach of securing IoMT against the new cyber threats. In this system, machine learning frameworks are trained on a highly performing level i.e. Random Forest, Extra Trees, Decision Tree, MLP, and LSTM classifiers to study the main characteristics of the network flow i.e. flow duration, length of packets, idle time and transmission rate. The models are trained in a manner that they may distinguish normal and abnormal traffic patterns such that it is able to adequately detect potential attacks in real time. The web page is developed on a Flask web framework with MySQL database integration in such a way that, users can easily register, upload network traffic data, choose prediction model and see the result. Once the abnormal patterns have been detected, the system will give actionable security insights and compliance recommendations, including the initiation of an investigation or ongoing monitoring. The platform is developed to have a simple interface, enabling even the non-technical users to communicate with highly developed AI-driven tools. This system can be used to make medical networks resilient by automating threat monitoring and providing contextual solutions, which can be applied to the overall critical infrastructure protection.

title: IAC-TecMed Database for Infrared Thermography-Based Diabetic Foot Monitoring
authors: Badiaa Ait Ahmed, Pilar Montañés-Rodríguez, Otman Aghzout, Juan Ruiz-Alzola
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This publication presents a publicly available subset of a database created to assess diabetic foot anomalies using infrared thermography, aimed at enhancing early detection of complications in diabetic patients. The initial study involved 22 healthy subjects (9 females and 13 males) to develop a standard morphological model for evaluating temperature asymmetry in the plantar aspects of both feet. The database comprises thermographic and RGB-D images captured at four time points (T0, T5, T10, T15) under controlled conditions (temperature: $\mathbf{2 5}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$, constant luminosity, and humidity), resulting in a longitudinal dataset that reflects natural thermal variations. This methodology is clinically relevant as it identifies thermal anomalies that may prevent complications in diabetic foot. Images were subjected to segmentation masks, manually delineated by three experienced researchers, and validated via the STAPLE algorithm to establish a ground truth. Statistical analyses utilized Wilcoxon’s matched-pairs test, with adjustments for multiple comparisons via Hochberg and false discovery rate methods, maintaining a significance level of 5%. These analyses revealed significant temperature variation areas, reinforcing the validity of the results. From the cohort, 19 participants (7 females and 12 males) consented to share their data for research. This subdataset (IAC-TecMed database, hereafter IAC-TMBD) is a valuable resource for exploring thermal patterns and their implications in foot disorder monitoring. This subset retains the integrity and scope of the original data, providing a resource for further investigation into temperature patterns and their implications for early detection and monitoring of foot disorders.

title: Investigating the Synergy between Industry 4.0 Technologies, Lean Tools, and Lean Wastes
authors: Rashid Alnaqbi, Zehra Canan Araci
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: The topic Lean 4.0 has gained an interest among researchers and practitioners in recent years as the tools and methods it provides can help organizations to improve the productivity of their processes and operations. Lean 4.0 is the integration of Lean principles and Industry 4.0 technologies. There seems to be a relationship between Lean principles and the digital technologies of Industry $\mathbf{4. 0}$. This research proposes investigating this relationship to understand how Lean tools and methods would synergize with Industry 4.0, in order to eliminate or reduce Lean wastes. The methodology used in the research is a systematic literature review and a bibliometric analysis. Understanding the relationship between Industry 4.0, Lean tool, and Lean wastes conjointly will be beneficial to organizations of different sizes and sectors, as it can provide insights and perspectives on how to tackle a specific waste or inefficiency in their processes and activities.

title: Rotor load decoupling method based on geometric modal projection
authors: Feng Cao, Lei Liang, Shu Dai, Weijian Zhong, Tianmingxuan Cao
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: To address modal decoupling failure and crosstalk caused by sensor installation deviations in rotor load measurement, a robust identification method based on geometric modal projection and stiffness correction is proposed. Unlike traditional methods, this approach eliminates the reliance on perfect physical symmetry by utilizing redundant information from multiple sensors to construct an observation matrix in modal space. Combined with load calibration, geometric installation deviations are automatically absorbed into an equivalent stiffness matrix. Simulation results for a nonlinear pre-twisted blade show that under a 50 mm chordwise deviation, the lead-lag load RMSE of the traditional bridge method reaches 578.47 Nm, while the proposed method maintains an RMSE of only 1.03 Nm. The identification error for sectional principal stiffness is only $\mathbf{0. 7 \%}$.

title: A Machine Learning-Assisted Approach for High-Precision PV Module Characterization
authors: Sanat Kumar Patro, Deepak Kumar Barik, Saumyaranjan Sahoo, Gunamani Jena
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: A machine learning-assisted approach for determining the parameters of a dual-diode photovoltaic module model is introduced, utilizing an enhanced differential evolution method with a two-phase mutation technique, referred to as Multimodal Multi-Objective Differential Evolution with Two-Stage Mutation and Modified Maximum Extension Distance (MMODE-TSM-MMED). This strategy is designed to enhance diversity and accelerate convergence. The effectiveness of the proposed technique is assessed by comparing the derived outcomes with those of alternative algorithms. Observations indicate that the optimal solution stabilizes at 6.682 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">-6</sup> across all iterations, with a maximum iteration count of fewer than 15.

title: Cloud-based Intelligent Brain Tumor Detection and Segmentation Framework using Hybrid Deep Representation Learning Approach
authors: K. Anitha, T. Priyaradhikadevi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: A brain tumor (BT) is an abnormal development in the brain, which destroys its functions and poses a major danger to human lifespan because of weakened neurons. Earlier recognition and identification of BTs are more important for averting health-related problems and sustaining better health conditions. Popular technologies like artificial intelligence and cloud computing are gradually making their way into the healthcare industry, enabling superior decision-making for radiologists. The technology of cloud computing aids in automatically analyzing BTs. Besides, recent progress in deep learning systems has exposed vast potential in image classification and segmentation for BT. This paper presents an Intelligent Cloud-Based Brain Tumor Detection and Segmentation Framework using a Hybrid Deep Learning Model (CBTDS-HDL). The major intention is to develop an advanced cloud-enabled system that can accurately perceive and visualize BTs from medical imaging. To perform that, the proposed CBTDS-HDL model initially pre-processes the input image using a Wiener filter (WF) for noise removal and CLAHE for contrast enhancement (CE) to enhance the image clarity and quality. For tumor segmentation, an R2UNet model optimized using the RMSProp optimizer is utilized to precisely determine tumor boundaries. Following that, the multi-scale vision transformer model that uses a base (B16) is employed for feature extraction to capture detailed information from segmented images. Finally, the tumor detection is carried out using a temporal convolutional bi-directional long short-term memory optimized with RMSProp, which enables accurate tumor classification. To show an improved performance of the proposed CBTDS-HDL method, a complete experimentation study is made. The comparative outcomes stated the improved performances of the CBTDS-HDL method with maximum accuracy of 96.65%.

title: Energy-Efficient AI Models for Large-Scale Technical Data Collection in Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Kiran Saripudi
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: Large-scale Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) produce diverse, high-velocity technical data that necessitate constant acquisition, processing, and transmission. When using traditional fixed-rate data collection methods, energy is wasted, communication is slowed down, and sampling is done too often, especially in distributed sensor networks. This paper presents an energy-efficient AI-driven data acquisition framework that incorporates Reinforcement Learning (RL), adaptive sampling, and lightweight deep learning models to dynamically optimize sampling rates according to sensed context, signal variability, and operational constraints. When tested on a publicly avaialble data set Intel Lab Data with 120 sensor nodes used to monitor temperature, the proposed model uses 41.6 % less energy, 37.2 % fewer redundant data samples, and 22.4 % more accurate data than static sampling. The framework shows promise for being used on a large scale in smart grids, the industrial Internet of Things, precision agriculture, and strong CPS infrastructures.

title: Quaternary Reversible Circuit Optimization for Scalable Multiplexer and Demultiplexer
authors: Y. Sunanda, M. Ravi Kishore, K. Shankar, Y. Pavan Kumar Reddy, P. Syamala Devi, P. Mounika
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: Reversible computing is starting to establish itself as a practical solution for the energy dissipation problem in digital systems and is anticipated to be the future by pushing the limits of information loss. There are certain areas where quaternary reversible logic (multi-valued reversible logic) has an edge over irreversible binary (two-valued reversible logic) including information density, interconnect complexity, and overall scalability. We describe the design and implementation of a quaternary reversible Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) which contains large functional units and which operates like those in the modern ALU's; offering such functions as arithmetic, logic, memory, decoders, and multiplexers. The system will be affecting the movement of data and control of operation to scale (like a flexible control unit) and even adopts the use of a selection-line design. The proposed ALU shows some major advances in quantum cost, garbage output, and steady-state inputs in comparison to earlier designs, and yet, it can be a compatible one to design scalability for larger reversible and quantum computing systems overall. The achieved results through simulations and comparisons indicate that the proposed system is more efficient and has a significantly lower power consumption than the already proposed designs; therefore, it is reasonable to say that it could be incorporated into the future of computing technologies

title: Follow the Sun and Catch the Wind: A Spatio-Temporal GNN-DRL Routing Framework for Carbon-Neutral Geo-Distributed Data Centers
authors: Alicia Chai, Page Zhang, Xuehan Sun
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Power System Engineering and Smart Grid (PSESG)
abstract: The explosive growth of cloud computing and Large Language Model (LLM) training has propelled data centers to become primary consumers of global electricity. While temporal workload shifting within a single data center can partially align compute demand with renewable energy availability, its efficacy is fundamentally constrained by local meteorological volatility and strict Service Level Agreements (SLAs). In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift from temporal to spatio-temporal scheduling by exploiting geo-distributed data centers. We introduce a "Follow the Sun, Follow the Wind" spatial routing framework that dynamically migrates computational workloads to global regions currently experiencing abundant, low-carbon renewable energy generation. However, migrating data across continents incurs significant Wide Area Network (WAN) bandwidth costs and transmission latency. To resolve this complex multi-objective trade-off, we formulate the network as a dynamic graph and propose a novel Spatio-Temporal Graph Proximal Policy Optimization (STG-PPO) algorithm. The framework utilizes Graph Neural Networks (GNN) to capture the topological correlations of global renewable energy grids and WAN congestion, empowering a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) agent to execute microsecond-level routing decisions. Comprehensive experiments on real-world Google Cluster traces and multi-continent NREL weather data demonstrate that our STG-PPO approach reduces global carbon emissions by 41.2% compared to state-of-the-art temporal schedulers, while strictly bounding WAN transmission latency and bandwidth expenditures, offering a robust blueprint for global sustainable smart city infrastructures.

title: Mitigating Misalignment of Excitation Sources in Low-Frequency Mammography
authors: Ghazaleh Tashtarian, Hamid Akbari-Chelaresi, Mauricio Hernandez, Farzan Mirshekari, Ahad Tavakoli, Omar M. Ramahi
publish: 2026 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
abstract: Any movement of the transmitter antenna during breast tumor detection can cause localization errors. This paper presents a technique to mitigate misalignment and accurately detect and localize tumors in a 2-D impression. The proposed method employs cross-correlation and Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) analysis, and simulation results confirm its effectiveness.

title: Deep Learning-based Smart Traffic Signal Control
authors: T Thilagam, Siva Rama Lingham N, T Praveen Kumar, Ritish Patro
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The Indian cities have very complicated traffic control processes because of high density of roads, the vehicles mixed in terms of their types, unpredictable drivers, and the varying amount of traffic at different times of the day. The traditional fixed-time or sensor-based traffic systems are not able to respond to such dynamic conditions, and it results in the increase of congestion and waiting time. To solve this problem, the proposed research suggests an intelligent traffic control system that combines both an LSTM network to predict short-term traffic and a Q-learning system to optimize the signal phases in real-time. LSTM model predicts the impending traffic density and traffic flow in the near future according to the historical records, whereas the Q-learning optimizes signal timing to reduce delays and traffic congestion. The Kaggle Urban Traffic Dataset and IEEE Indian Vehicle Dataset are used to train the model and capture the nature of the Indian traffic. The application of performance evaluation through the SUMO simulator shows that there is a 32 percent decrease in the average vehicle waiting time and a 19 percent increase in efficiency of the traffic flow. Also, the system has the priority of emergency vehicles as it allows free circulation of intersection clearance without affecting the safety. The proposed deep learning-based framework can be applied to the field of urban mobility to the benefit of the Indian urban environment, by predicting congestion proactively and optimizing signals dynamically, to enhance the mobility of the Indian population in the city.

title: Optimized Microenvironments for Battery Cell Production: Space-Efficient Handling Systems and Economic Feasibility
authors: Johannes Scholz, Jan-Niklas Sturm, Florian Kößler, Jürgen Fleischer
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Production Technologies and Systems for E-Mobility (EPTS)
abstract: The increasing demand for battery cells, driven by market volatility and rapid technological advancements, necessitates agile and energy-efficient production strategies. Traditional large-scale dry rooms, which are essential for moisture-sensitive battery manufacturing, pose significant challenges due to their high energy consumption and limited adaptability. As an alternative, microenvironments as small dry rooms designed as robot cells, offer a more flexible and efficient solution by creating localized, controlled atmospheres that minimize energy usage while maintaining strict environmental conditions. A key advantage of microenvironments is the elimination of human operators within these enclosed spaces, requiring fully automated handling processes. However, standard industrial robots, often employed for automation, demand substantial space, leading to an increase in the overall volume of the microenvironment and, consequently, higher operational costs. To address this limitation, the microenvironment is analysed systematically to identify the interfaces and functions of the microenvironment. Based on the functional description, a space-optimized handling system is developed that enables material manipulation while minimizing the required footprint. This study explores the design and implementation of such a system, using a dry coating process as a practical application example. Beyond the technical development, the economic viability of microenvironments and the proposed handling system is assessed. By evaluating energy savings, material efficiency, and operational flexibility, the study highlights the potential of microenvironments to enhance the sustainability and competitiveness of battery cell production. The findings suggest that integrating compact, automated handling solutions within microenvironments can significantly reduce costs and improve adaptability to fluctuating market conditions. The paper presents the potential of an optimized handling system inside microenvironments regarding the required space. Furthermore, a standardized functional description of microenvironments is given as a basis for the design of optimized microenvironments.

title: An AI-Driven Cyber-Physical Control System for Real-Time Environmental Monitoring and Disaster Management
authors: Sorabh Lakhanpal, Navneet Kumar Rajpoot, Poornima Ediga, Manjula G, Dilraj Preet Kaur, Ashish Kumar Singhal
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: This paper presents a proposed AI-based Cyber-Physical Control System (AI-CPCS), which is used to monitor the environment and manage disasters in heterogeneous sensing infrastructures in real time. It will be designed with the help of multimodal IoT sensors, edge-intelligent computing nodes, and a blockchain-secured data integrity layer to provide credible and low-latency decision streams. The hybrid deep learning pipeline that uses spatiotemporal convolutional encoders and sequence predictors based on transformers are constructed to approximate environmental dynamics and identify abnormally deviant patterns emphasizing the signs of floods, landslides, and atmospheric instabilities. Distributed control loop uses reinforcement learning by automatically adjusting resource allocation, communication bandwidth, and actuation feedback as the environmental loads change. The system has a priority-based message routing protocol that minimizes the end-to-end delay with resilience to node failure. Synthetic testing of a multi-hazard benchmark shows a vastly better predictive performance, event capture ability, and control responsiveness than traditional CPS models. The suggested AI-CPCS creates a scalable base of the autonomous disaster mitigation systems of the next generation.

title:
Interface and Transport-Layer Engineering for CdS / BaZrS
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    / CuSCN Solar Cells: Device Simulation, Band Alignment and Defect Effects
authors: Abhijit Kumar Kar, Mnisha, Chandni Tiwari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: This simulation study provides an important blueprint for the development of high-efficiency, lead-free BaZrS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> perovskite solar cells. Through a thorough SCAPS-1D analysis of a FTO/CdS/BaZrS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>/CuSCN/Au stack, we determine that the most important levers of performance are interface defect passivation and the precise band alignments. The front HTL/absorber interface (IDL1) is quite sensitive to defects, showing very large voltage and fill factor losses for defect densities > 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup> cm⁻<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>, while the rear ETL/absorber interface (IDL2) limits the current for higher MT (defect density). Our simulations predict that a peak power-conversion efficiency of 16.77% is possible with the best designs, using a 700-800 nm thick absorber, very small interface traps (IDL1 ≤ 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">11</sup> -10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup> cm⁻<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>) and with the band structure tuned so as to have a small positive conduction band offset at the ETL interface and an HTL work function of 5.05-5.15 eV. We conclude that the experimental investigations necessary will need to accelerate passivation of the front interface and fine-tuning of the absorber processing necessary in order to bring defect densities down to the bulk levels of below 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">13</sup> cm⁻<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>. This work will translate the innately large promise of the BaZrS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> system into more precise, actionable targets for the realization of high performance, non-toxic perovskite photovoltaics.

title: Deep Hybrid Architecture for Cybersecurity Threat Detection in IIOT Networks: CNN–BiGRU Approach
authors: Rajni Jindal, Valluri Daneesha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: The rapid growth of Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) technologies has transformed industrial automation, manufacturing, and cyber-physical systems. However, the increased connectivity among different devices creates significant cybersecurity risks. Traditional intrusion detection systems struggle to manage the dynamic and large amounts of data generated in IIoT settings. This paper presents a Deep Hybrid Architecture that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Units (BiGRU) to effectively detect cybersecurity threats in IIoT networks. CNNs efficiently pull spatial features from packet-level data, while BiGRU layers capture bidirectional temporal dependencies in industrial data streams. Tests on the UNSW-NB15 dataset show notable improvements in performance by class: our model achieves a 70% increase in F1-score for backdoor attacks, a 246% improvement in recall for backdoor detection, and a 10-12% increase for shellcode. Additionally, it shows a 7.4% improvement for reconnaissance and a 30% improvement for DOS attacks. Overall accuracy improved by 3.02%, climbing from 71.81% to 74.83%. The significant gains for rare and stealthy attack types—which present the highest risk in industrial settings—confirm that modeling temporal context is vital for identifying evolving IIoT intrusions. The CNN-BiGRU architecture improves generalization without substantially increasing computational demand (a 12% rise in training time), maintains over 96% accuracy for benign traffic, and positions itself as an effective, scalable, and context-aware intrusion detection solution ideal for real-time IIoT cybersecurity.

title: Deep Learning-Based Prediction of Pulsed Electric Field and Biological Parameters from In Vitro Experiments
authors: Nicolò Colistra, Arianna Casciati, Simone Samperna, Raffaele Crusi, Francesca Camera, Mirella Tanori, Luciano Tarricone, Marco Zappatore, Caterina Merla
publish: 2026 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
abstract: Pulsed Electric Fields (PEFs) have emerged as a minimally invasive and highly controllable therapeutic modality for cancer treatment, capable of inducing reversible or irreversible electroporation depending on pulse parameters. Recent advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI) offer new opportunities to optimize PEF protocols by exploiting complex, nonlinear relationships between electrical parameters, cellular properties, and biological outcomes. In this work, we present a novel AI-based framework for the optimization of standalone PEF therapeutic protocols. Unlike standard approaches relying on literature-derived parameter selection, the proposed methodology is grounded on experimental in vitro data obtained from six human tumor cell lines with distinct molecular profiles exposed to various PEF conditions. Multiple Artificial Neural Network (ANN) models were specifically developed to predict cell viability and proliferation and optimize the electrical signal characteristics. The results demonstrate the ability of AI-driven models to accurately capture PEF-induced cellular responses and to guide the selection of effective, tumor-specific therapeutic protocols, paving the way toward data-driven and personalized oncological therapies.

title: An Efficient Tuberculosis Detection from Chest X-ray Images using Lightweight Deep Learning Models
authors: K.V.D.S. Santhosh, Jayadeep Pakala, Songa Manikumar, K.V Mounika, A.Lakshmanarao, P.Chandra Sekhar Reddy
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Tuberculosis (TB) remains a major global health concern, and chest X-ray imaging is widely used for large-scale TB screening due to its accessibility and low cost. However, manual interpretation of chest X-rays is time-consuming and subject to observer variability, motivating the need for reliable automated detection systems. This paper presents a progressive learning framework for automated tuberculosis detection from chest X-ray images, systematically transitioning from classical machine learning to deep and transfer learning paradigms under a unified experimental setup. Initially, handcrafted texture features are classified using conventional machine learning models to establish baseline performance, followed by an artificial neural network to capture nonlinear feature relationships. A custom convolutional neural network is then employed to learn spatial representations directly from images. Finally, a proposed lightweight transfer learning framework utilizing MobileNetV2, ShuffleNetV2, and EfficientNetB0 is introduced to achieve higher accuracy with reduced computational complexity. Experimental results on a publicly available tuberculosis chest X-ray dataset demonstrate consistent performance improvements across learning stages, with lightweight transfer learning models achieving the best balance between diagnostic accuracy and efficiency. The proposed framework offers a practical and effective solution for automated TB screening, particularly suitable for deployment in resource-constrained healthcare environments.

title: BIM–AI Integration for Sustainable Urban Planning in Smart Cities: Framework, Challenges, and Future Trends
authors: Noorsan Abu Ja’far, Mohammad Alauthman, Amer Al-Jokhadar, Ali Al Maqusi
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Progress toward Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 11 depends on planning processes that connect building-level performance to city-level dynamics. Building Information Modelling (BIM) provides semantically rich, quantitative descriptions of assets, yet these models are commonly retained within project repositories and are not systematically used in municipal workflows. Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and 3D city models contribute spatial context at urban scale, but they often lack the granularity required to evaluate embodied carbon, energy demand, accessibility, and resilience at the level of individual buildings. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI)—including graph learning, large language models, and neuro-symbolic approaches—make it increasingly feasible to integrate these sources and implement urban digital twins that can support policy formulation and design evaluation. This paper presents a governance-aware conceptual framework that integrates BIM, GIS/CityGML, and urban sensing through a layered pipeline for data ingestion, semantic integration, AI analytics, and SDG-aligned decision support. In lieu of a prototype, the paper reviews work reported during 2021–2025 and critically examines persistent barriers and emerging directions, including semantic interoperability, privacy-preserving data governance, generative urban design, and automated SDG compliance checking. Because data availability varies widely across cities, we also discuss data-readiness tiers and fallback strategies for contexts where BIM, IoT streams, or semantic 3D city models are incomplete, fragmented, or unavailable.

title: Evaluating the Importance of Marine Ecological Protection: A Case Study of the Dalian Sea Area, Liaoning Province
authors: Wenhu Lu, Long He, Lu Yang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: Marine ecological protection is one of the global common concerns. Scientific identification of conservation priority areas and the evaluation of the importance of ecological protection is one of the important routes to achieve the best protection effect with limited investment. In this study, taking the sea area of Dalian City, Liaoning Province as an example, a qualitative and quantitative evaluation index system and method for the importance of marine ecological protection were constructed. Through the criteria of the importance of marine ecosystem service and ecological vulnerability, conservation priority areas were identified in Dalian, and their relative importance levels were evaluated. Results showed that the area of extremely important ecological protection regions was approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6,867 \text{km}^{2}$</tex>, accounting for 22.8% of the total sea area of Dalian, including the distribution areas of Phoca largha and key protected or threatened seabirds, distribution areas of aquatic germplasm resources, seagrass meadows, estuaries and saltmarshes. The area of important ecological protection regions was approximately 16,679 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{km}^{2}$</tex>, accounting for 55.54% of total sea area of Dalian, including tidal flats and shallow sea waters, migration channels and winter grounds for fishery resources. The evaluation of the importance of marine ecological protection is the most fundamental work for the delimitation of marine ecological red line and ecological zone. The research can provide scientific guidance for marine ecological zoning and marine biodiversity conservation.

title: RE-FIDS: Robust and Explainable Federated Learning-based Intrusion Detection System
authors: Pranav Prajapati, Atharva Karambe, Ruchi Kharab, Nilesh Mane, Parikshit Singh, Sunita Dhavale
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Modern cyber-threats continue to evolve across distributed infrastructures where centralized Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) face critical challenges related to data privacy, scalability, and localized reliability. Federated Learning (FL) offers a decentralized alternative for collaborative model training; however, traditional FL-IDS frameworks remain vulnerable to Non-IID data distributions, gradient poisoning attacks, and a lack of decision-making transparency. To address these limitations, we propose RE-FIDS, a Robust and Explainable Federated Intrusion Detection System. Our methodology integrates Dirichlet-based heterogeneous data modeling with a multi-stage Hybrid Aggregator that combines Krum filtering and Trimmed Mean smoothing to neutralize adversarial updates. Furthermore, we incorporate a SHAP-based interpretability layer to provide feature-level transparency. Empirical evaluations on the CIC-IDS2017 and UNSW-NB15 datasets demonstrate that RE-FIDS significantly outperforms FedAvg and standalone robust aggregators under 20% poisoning pressure. The framework achieves stable convergence and superior F1-scores, effectively preserving model integrity in hostile environments. This study provides a scalable solution for secure, interpretable network monitoring, bridging the gap between high-performance anomaly detection and operational trust in decentralized security operations centers.

title: Intracavity Temperature Sensor Based on Phase-Shifted Fiber Bragg Grating in a Fiber Laser
authors: Boqiang Lin, Yihua He, Renan Xu, Weihao Lin
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: This paper proposes and validates a temperature sensing system based on phase-shifted fiber Bragg grating (PSFBG) for monitoring minor temperature changes within the range of $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. In the experiment, the PSFBG was integrated as both a filtering and sensing element inside the fiber laser cavity. As the ambient temperature changed, the laser’s output wavelength exhibited linear drift. The experimental results show that the sensing system has a sensitivity of $8 \mathrm{pm} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Additionally, the output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the laser cavity exceeded 50 dB, and the laser linewidth was less than 0.1 nm. This sensor demonstrates promising potential for precise temperature monitoring in biomedical applications.

title: Time-Series Forecasting of Cloud Resource Utilization Using Hybrid LSTM Models
authors: Vijayachandar Sanikal
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: Cloud resource utilization time-series forecasting is highly important in smart workload management and cost-effective cloud operations. Broadly speaking in the field of cloud intelligence and predictive computing, the accurate forecasting has been created useful in proactive scaffolding, SLA assurance, and energy-conscientious decision-making. The proposed work presents a new Hybrid LSTM forecasting model that combines stacked temporal learning, attentive refinements with a residual correction unit to increase the prediction strength. This model is successful in capturing long-range temporal correlated effects, dominant utilization patterns, and intra does not force the cumulative error drift in the dynamic workloads. The experiments that have been carried out using actual cloud workload traces prove that the suggested framework performs significantly better as compared to ARIMA, GRU, basic LSTM, and CNN Hybrids of LSTM. The model records significant improvements in performance, such as: reduction in RMSE, MAE, and MAPE; extending R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>, forecast stability, reducing inference latency and robustness in the face of noisy fluctuations in workload. Altogether, the suggested Hybrid LSTM based forecasting model offers a more credible, accurate and deployable solution to the problem of cloud utilization prediction, supporting intelligent autoscaling, performance optimization and sustainable cloud resource management.

title: Harnessing Large Language Models for Next-Generation Artificial Intelligence Applications
authors: Navin Garg, Sasikanth Yangalasetty, Tharakesavulu Vangalapat, Mina Kumari, Mahesh Basavaraj, Manish Gupta
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) usage is the innovation capable of introducing the new generation of the artificial intelligence system with adaptive thinking, multimodal understanding, and grounded knowledge synthesis. Transformer architecture can be developed; less supervised scalable cognitive automation can be enabled by combining developed augmented retrieval-based generation and parameter-efficient tuning. This paper presents a technically valid and conceptually coherent hybrid-grounded LLM model that has cross-modal embedding alignment, external-knowledge recall, symbolical equivalent validation, and, the hallucination-controlled decoding. The methodology guarantees the semantic integrity and logic-sensitive decision-support in the different fields. The experimental evaluation of a group of complex reasoning demonstrates a considerable improvement as the number of perplexities was decreased to 7.1 instead of 16.8, the level of overall accuracy was increased by 63.4 to 83.8 percent, and the level of F1-score improved by 65.1 to 85.4. Generative metrics also show a significant improvement as BLEU scores 36.9, ROUGE-L scores 56.4 and exact match scores also goes to 63.2 and expectation calibration error also goes down to 3.6 and hallucination error also drops to 5.1. These findings indicate that grounded LLM design is an experimental catalyst of the future AI ecosystems with central interests on reliability, knowledge validity and high-fidelity reason.

title: An AI-Driven Optimization Framework for Sustainable E-Waste Management and Resource Utilization
authors: Priyanka Chauhan, Deepak Kumar Adhana, Mayank Saxena
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: In the given paper, an AI-based optimization model of sustainable e-waste management and resource use is presented. The research falls in the fields of circular economy engineering, intelligent decision analytics, and intelligent urban sustainability systems. The framework creates a spatio-temporal digital twin of e-waste flows, which incorporates graph neural prediction of waste generation, deep reinforcement learning prediction of adaptive collection and routing, and multi-objective evolutionary optimization prediction of processing and material recovery. Lifecycle sustainability assessment is incorporated to pursue, all at the same time, cost, environmental impact, and circularity goals. Experimental assessment on the city scale shows that there are very high increments in performances relative to traditional methods that depend on rules to use in collection, logistics systems relying on optimization and, hybrid AI logistics systems. The efficiency of the proposed framework is 93% material recovery efficiency, 23.1% reduction on greenhouse gases, 25.6% operational costs efficiency and 27.4% reduction on waste which is not controlled. Further measurements ensure better routing effectiveness, increased service completion, greater prediction performance, increased processing throughput, greater circularity gain, and greater stability of policy compliance. These findings prove the potential of the framework to convert e-waste ecosystems into smart, resilient, and sustainability-oriented resource infrastructures of the contemporary smart cities.

title: Research on Construction and Virtual Commissioning of Intelligent Warehousing System Based on PQFactory Platform
authors: Runsheng Hao, Guohao Feng
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: Digital twin virtual simulation technology plays a significant role in the virtual commissioning of intelligent production lines. Based on the PQFactory platform, this paper conducts virtual commissioning research on the intelligent warehousing system design. A digital model of the warehousing system is designed, a digital twin scenario is built on the PQFactory platform, and relevant parameters are configured according to the process flow. The robot program and PLC control program are compiled, and the KingIOServer gateway is used to realize communication between the physical PLC and the PQFactory platform, completing the integrated digital twin commissioning of the entire system. The virtual simulation verifies the accuracy and reliability of the intelligent warehousing system design, which helps improve the design and commissioning efficiency and reduce the development cycle and cost.

title: New approach to improve low-noise amplifier performances for 2.45 GHz wireless applications
authors: Moustapha Séré, Jamal Zbitou
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This article describes a new approach to improving the performance of low-noise amplifiers (LNA) operating at 2.45 GHz. It focuses on the use of novel circuit topologies, advanced techniques to reduce the noise figure (NF), and simultaneous gain enhancement. Such improvement is made possible by advancements in CMOS technologies, such as gallium arsenide (GaAs), and the careful selection of impedance matching network components. The GaAs-based ATF-21170 transistor is used as the active element in the proposed LNA. This LNA operates in class $\mathbf{A B}$ and is designed using the Advanced Design System (ADS) software. Simulations yield a gain of 15.918 dB and a noise figure of $\mathbf{1. 1 4 3 ~ d B}$. The input (S11) and output (S22) reflection coefficients are -20.136 dB and -15.205 dB, respectively. The implementation layout measures $\mathbf{4 0} \times \mathbf{8 3. 5} \mathbf{~ m m}^{\mathbf{2}}$. The final results demonstrate enhanced performance for receivers used in wireless communications, such as radar systems, Wi-Fi, and satellite communications…

title: FDSCBAM-YOLO: Integrating Frequency-Domain Attention with Depthwise Separable Convolution for Data-Scarce SAR Vehicle Detection
authors: Dan Gao, Shuo Yu, Caoyi Mei, Xiaofang Wu, Zhijin Wen
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: The intelligent advancement of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) is frequently hindered by the scarcity of high-quality annotated data. To address this fundamental challenge, we propose FDSCBAM-YOLO, a lightweight detection network incorporating a novel Frequency-domain Depthwise Separable Convolutional Attention Module (FDSCBAM). By synergistically integrating depthwise separable convolutions with both frequency-domain and spatial attention mechanisms, FDSCBAM effectively suppresses inherent coherent speckle noise and accentuates target scattering features in the frequency domain without compromising computational efficiency. Furthermore, we introduce a frequency-domain enhanced loss function specifically tailored to improve small target detection.
