This paper presents an advanced deep learning (DL) architecture capable of identifying and localizing lesions in gastroscopic images for the diagnosis of early gastric cancer (EGC). The study integrates state-of-the-art feature extraction (FE) methods with a simplified feature selection process to enhance diagnostic precision and effectiveness. The publicly available Kvasir-SEG dataset was used, and the input images were preprocessed through resizing, filtering, and noise reduction to improve image quality. The annotated images were then detected and segmented using the trained and validated optimized DL model. The results of the comparative evaluation revealed that the proposed method outperformed existing methods in terms of key performance metrics, such as precision, accuracy, and the Dice coefficient. These results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively outline lesion boundaries and distinguish between pathological and normal gastric tissues. The findings indicate that the optimized DL model is a useful and reliable architecture for identifying early gastric cancer and can be successfully applied in real-time clinical practice and decision support in gastroenterology.

title: Ocular Disease Classification and Diabetic Retinopathy Detection using Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Malti Rani, Amit Kumar Goyal, Sonali Dash
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: A severe complication of diabetes that leads to permanently vision loss and early blindness give rise to the evolution of an ocular disease called as diabetic retinopathy. The major root cause of diabetic retinopathy is prolonged hyperglycemia that generally weakens the tiny retinal capillaries. The poor tiny retinal capillaries give rise to the development of microaneurysms and can completely block the flow of blood in the retinal vessels. This damaged retinal blood vessels induce the eruption of vascular endothelial growth factor VEGF which leads to the development of new fragile retinal blood vessels known as neovascularization. For this ocular diagnosing, fundus images play a crucial role. This paper represents a deep learning approach used for classification of diabetic retinopathy and normal fundus images from ODIR dataset integrating them with homomorphic filtering. Two convolutional neural network architecture, MobileNetV2 and VGG16 are hyper-tuned for image classification and disease detection. The final outcomes revealed better feature extraction and improved classification performance as the application of homomorphic filtering leads to enhanced contrast and illumination uniformly over the fundus images.

title: Constraint-Aware Hybrid Metaheuristic Optimization for Joint IDS Placement and Key-Refresh Scheduling in Resource-Constrained IoT Edge Networks
authors: Hasan Kanaker, Samer A. B. Awwad
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Security controls in Internet-of-Things (IoT) deployments must operate under tight energy, memory, and latency budgets, making how and where security is enforced an optimization problem rather than a fixed design choice. This paper formulates a joint IoT security optimization that selects (i) the placement of lightweight intrusion detection system (IDS) agents on edge nodes and (ii) per-device cryptographic key-refresh periods, while assigning devices to deployed IDS agents under capacity and distance constraints. The resulting mixed-integer nonlinear program is NP-hard due to combinatorial placement/assignment and nonlinear risk/overhead terms. To solve it, we propose a constraint-aware hybrid optimizer (CHPDE) that couples binary particle swarm optimization (BPSO) for placement with differential evolution (DE) operators for continuous key-refresh variables and a repair mechanism for feasibility. Using a synthetic IoT-edge benchmark generator, we provide theoretical performance tables and plots comparing genetic algorithm (GA), PSO, DE, and grey wolf optimizer (GWO) against CHPDE. Theoretical results indicate that CHPDE converges faster and achieves lower objective values with improved security-risk reduction under the same compute budget.

title: Microwave Hyperthermia Treatment Planning Via Combination of Focused Patterns and Low-Dimensional Global Optimization
authors: Sabrina Zumbo, Tommaso Isernia, Martina Teresa Bevacqua
publish: 2026 IEEE MTT-S International Microwave Biomedical Conference (IMBioC)
abstract: Electromagnetic field shaping plays a central role in microwave hyperthermia, where energy must be deposited selectively inside a tumor while limiting heating in healthy tissues. This paper introduces a reduced-order approach in which the field is synthesized as a linear combination of a small number of time-reversal focused patterns defined at some selected control points inside the target. The corresponding complex weights are optimized through a global optimization algorithm under field-intensity constraints. The method is preliminarily tested against a heterogeneous scenario mimicking a human breast at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 1 5 ~ M H z}$</tex>.

title: High-Gain 24 GHz Microstrip Patch Antenna Array Using a Corporate Wilkinson Power Divider
authors: Mohamed Guermal, Jamal Zbitou, Fouad Aytouna, Stephane Ginestar, Mohammed El Gibari
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This paper presents the design and simulation of a 32element microstrip patch antenna array operating at 24 GHz for millimeter-wave applications. The proposed array is designed on a Rogers RT5880 substrate. A compact Wilkinson power divider is employed to achieve uniform power distribution with excellent impedance matching and high port isolation. The simulated results demonstrate a reflection coefficient better than $\mathbf{- 3 0 ~ d B}$ for the single patch antenna, while the Wilkinson divider exhibits S11 below - 19 dB, equal power split of - 3 dB, and an isolation level of -20 dB between output ports. When integrated into the complete 32-element antenna array, the system maintains a strong impedance matching with a return loss around $-\mathbf{2 5 ~ d B}$ at $\mathbf{2 4 ~ G H z}$. The radiation characteristics confirm high directivity and improved gain, making the proposed antenna array suitable for automotive radar, millimeter-wave communications, and highfrequency wireless systems.

title: A Comparative Analysis of Multi-Head Self-Attention and Axial Attention in Transformer Architectures
authors: Amine Berquedich, Lahbib Ajallouda, Ahmed Zellou
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Transformer architectures have become foundational models across natural language processing, computer vision, and multimodal learning, primarily due to the effectiveness of self-attention mechanisms. However, the quadratic computational complexity of standard self-attention limits scalability for high-dimensional inputs, particularly in vision tasks requiring high-resolution processing. Axial attention has emerged as a promising alternative that factorizes attention along individual axes to improve efficiency while maintaining global receptive fields. This paper presents a comprehensive comparative analysis of Multi-Head SelfAttention and Axial Attention, examining their theoretical foundations, computational properties, representational capacity, and practical trade-offs across domains. Through empirical evaluation on medical image segmentation using a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network-Transformer architecture, we demonstrate that while MHSA achieves marginally higher accuracy, Axial Attention provides substantial computational efficiency gains, enabling processing of higher resolution inputs with significantly reduced memory footprint. Our analysis provides practical guidelines for selecting appropriate attention mechanisms based on task requirements, computational constraints, and input characteristics.

title: An analysis study of the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) to improve the management of colic in horses: Survival Prediction, Decision Support, and Automated Monitoring
authors: Karima Handani, Abdelhadi Assir
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Horse colic remains a leading cause of mortality and economic loss in veterinary medicine, primarily due to its complex etiology and the inherent subjectivity of traditional diagnostic methods. This study offers a thorough review of the body of research on artificial intelligence’s (AI) revolutionary role in filling in these diagnostic gaps. Four main thematic axes found in the state of the art serve as the foundation for the study. It first analyzes documented methods for preparing clinical data, focusing on how imputation and class balancing affect model reliability. Second, the review compares the performance of various algorithmic architectures reported in the literature and synthesizes findings on survival prediction. Third, it explores at how AI can be used as a decision support tool, emphasizing how crucial Explainable AI (XAI) is to building clinical trust and making real-world bedside applications possible. Finally, the study investigates developments in wearable sensors and computer vision for automated pain assessment and early distress detection. This study shows that AI offers an unbiased support system to enhance clinical intuition, ultimately leading to better therapeutic outcomes and horse welfare, by combining data from recent studies.

title: Real-Time Vision-based Vehicle Speed Estimation using YOLOv8 and Enhanced Deep SORT
authors: P. Dhanalakshmi, S.Lakshmi Narayana Reddy, Shaik Baji, S.Mohammed Saleem, T.Chethak Sai Kumar, G. Sreevalli
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: In this paper, a real-time vision-based vehicle speed estimation will be proposed to combine the effective use of YOLOv8 to detect vehicles and a superior Deep SORT tracking system to maintain the identity of vehicles. A single-stage detector called YOLOv8 can be used to perform the localization of objects with a more accurate result and with lower inference latency thereby being used in areas where real-time traffic monitoring is required. The Deep SORT algorithm is made more efficient to track vehicles in all the frames by attaching motion prediction and appearance-based embeddings to ensure that the same vehicle is tracked in different frames. A distance-time formulation computed vehicle speed based on tracked trajectories is used to identify overspeed and then vehicles are detected. Using experimental results, the proposed framework is proved to have high detection accuracy and much faster inference speed than traditional two-stage detectors.

title: CAP-FL: A Context-Aware Personalized Federated Learning Framework for Next-Word Prediction
authors: S. Ramya, Swathi K, Thamil Vasanth G K, Tharani G
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: In modern smart text interfaces, Next-Word Prediction (NWP) processes play an important part. However, the traditional approach to training such systems was done in a centralized location, which has created serious concerns related to user privacy and system scalability. Federated Learning system scalability. Federated Learning (FL) is a new technology that solves these challenges by allowing people to work together on training models to create new smart text interfaces without ever sharing raw data. While FL improves privacy preservation, conventional FL operations are not yet effective when working with Non-Independent and Identically Distributed (Non-IID) client datasets. The Context-Aware and Personalized Federated Learning (CAP-FL) process presented in this paper is an innovative concept for developing a personalized next-word prediction model based on GPT-2 inside a federated structure. It employs a two-tiered personalization process that includes: (1) Explicit Feature-Based Clustering, which groups clients with the same text style or genre, and (2) Context Token Injection (CTI), which prepends structured metadata tokens (e.g., play, genre, scene, and character) before the input sequences to enhance contextual conditioning. The experimental results obtained from testing with the Shakespeare Non-IID dataset demonstrate that CAP-FL is superior to existing standard FL algorithms such as FedAvg and FedProx, achieving lower cross-entropy loss, reduced perplexity, and higher prediction accuracy. In conclusion, CAP-FL is scalable, interpretable, and privacy-preserving, while supporting personalized text generation in federated environments. Future work will explore adaptive parameter tuning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods such as LoRA to further reduce communication overhead.

title: Transient Synchronization Stability Analysis of a Grid-connected VSC Based on Black-box Model
authors: Bixing Ren, Dajiang Wang, Jianhui Meng, Zheng Li, Dandan Zhu, Wenbo Li
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Power System Engineering and Smart Grid (PSESG)
abstract: Transient synchronization instability is a critical issue affecting the secure grid integration of renewable energy. The control models and parameters of actual converters are often unknown, making it difficult to effectively assess transient synchronization stability. To address this, this paper investigates the transient synchronization stability assessment of converters under black-box model conditions. First, model of the grid-connected converter retaining the PLL dynamics is established, and the key factors influencing transient synchronization stability are identified from the perspective of the equal area criterion. Subsequently, a key parameter identification method based on disturbance testing is proposed, which, combined with the equal area criterion, enables the assessment of converter transient synchronization stability under black-box conditions. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through case studies.

title: Cost-Sensitive Machine Learning for Cloud Network Intrusion Detection
authors: Louay Karadsheh, Faten Hamad, Hussam Fakhouri
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Detecting malicious activity in cloud networks is a critical defensive task for security operations centres (SOCs). In practice, intrusion detection systems must achieve strong detection performance while keeping false alarms low enough to remain operationally actionable, since high alert volumes can overwhelm analysts and delay response. This paper presents a fully reproducible machine–learning pipeline for intrusion detection using the NSL-KDD benchmark dataset, comparing baseline classifiers—logistic regression, random forests, support vector machines, gradient boosting, and multi-layer perceptrons—under repeated stratified cross-validation with consistent preprocessing and hyperparameter handling. Evaluation goes beyond accuracy and includes metrics suited to imbalanced detection tasks, including precision, recall, F1-score, Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC), ROC-AUC, and PR-AUC. Beyond fixed decision thresholds, we introduce a threshold-optimised (and optionally cost-sensitive) decision rule for logistic regression that selects an operating point to explicitly trade detection against false alarms. Experiments show that a random forest achieves the strongest overall performance (highest F1-score and MCC), while threshold optimisation meaningfully improves the precision–recall balance of logistic regression, illustrating a practical mechanism for adapting simpler models to SOC alerting constraints. All code, figures, and LaTeX-ready tables are released to enable end-to-end reproducibility.

title: Smart Mining Helmet for Health Monitoring and Safety Assistance
authors: Shaik Thasleem Bhanu, Aakash S, Bharatwajar Pssv, Don Becon M
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Working conditions in the mines subject the workers to severe physical pressures and it is therefore important to identify the health conditions as quickly as possible in a bid to avoid the occurrence of fatal cases. In this paper, a Smart Mining Helmet is introduced that will have the ability to read the body temperature, respiration rate, and heart rate through integrated sensors via the Arduino Uno. The system diagnoses the first symptoms of heat stress, unconsciousness, breathing disorders, and uncharacteristic cardiac activity, so it is possible to provide first aid to oneself in the underground. In case of distress indicators, the helmet automatically switches on interventions, including cooling fog spray, wake-up buzers, and controlled nerve stimulation in order to revive or stabilize the miner. There is real-time vital knowledge that is shown on an LCD to achieve constant awareness. The given solution insurers and improves safety by offering immediate personal assistance prior to the arrival of external aid, which is a quality, efficient, and fast solution to the protection of miners.

title: An Ultra-High-Resolution Detection Framework for Micro-Scale PCB Surface Defects
authors: Salam Al-E’Mari, Yousef Sanjalawe, Fadi Fataftah
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Printed circuit boards (PCBs) are essential for today’s electronic systems. Flecks caused by surface defects can appear during the Manufacturing process and can profoundly degrade the reliability and performance of the end-product. Automated Engineering and inspection are needed to identify signs of surface polishing defects remaining on boards, including micro-scale impacts, their high-density distribution, and their strong resemblance to the background. Even with the most recent deep learning approaches and object detection systems, the results are still not guaranteed. Most computer-based methods still rely on means and vector-based numerical methods, yielding poor results for identifying ultra-small defects. The ultra-high-resolution detection framework, tailored to the identification of micro-scale defects on PCBs, is the approach presented in this paper. The approach is built on the principle of prioritizing high resolution. One example of the approach is the reconstruction of stride-4 feature maps. Another example is the micro-scale detection head designed to preserve fine-grained spatial resolution. Additionally, the design incorporates multi-scale feature fusion to collate disparate feature sets, producing a more robust outcome while maintaining the desired low computational cost. Extensive experiments are conducted on the public HRIPCB dataset to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. Quantitative results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a precision of 98.9%, a recall of 98.6%, an mAP@0.5 of 99.4%, and an mAP@0.5:0.95 of 63.8%, outperforming several state-of-the-art PCB defect detection approaches. Both quantitative and qualitative results confirm the superiority and practical applicability of the proposed framework for high-precision PCB surface inspection.

title: Analytical Modeling of Microstrip Antenna using Green House Analysis
authors: Rajesh Kumar Nema, Veena Upadhyay, Pramod Patel, Preeti Verma, Kavita Barahar, Amit Nema
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: In this paper presents, green house analysis computational method for self inductance, positive and negative mutual inductance of the design antenna with presence and absent of the ground plane. Analyzed the effect of ground plane on geometrical inductance of the antenna. The computation has done in a with consideration of U type radiating patch. The proposed computation of geometry is done using negative mutual coupling approach. The geometrical structure has configured by ground plan, driven element and top layer U-Slot director. Optimization of the self inductance, positive and negative mutual inductance has been done by green house optimization technique After optimization, the optimum antenna design achieved and it has provided good operating impedance Bandwidth. The proposed parasitic array antenna has designed with the appropriate dimensions and can be used for different wireless communication. The green house analysis can be applied in an X Band, Ku Band and C-Band wireless communication.

title: Icarus: High-Altitude Platform Station Navigation with LEO Satellites
authors: Will Barrett, Sharbel Kozhaya, Jennifer Sanderson, Joe Saroufim, Samer Hayek, Zaher M. Kassas
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine
abstract: The opportunistic exploitation of Ku-band downlink signals from Starlink and OneWeb low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites for high-altitude platform station (HAPS) navigation is demonstrated. Named after the Greek mythological figure Icarus, the HAPS consisted of a weather balloon carrying a radio frequency (RF) platform. The HAPS was launched in July 2024 from the Moriarty Municipal Airport, New Mexico, USA, landing near Mountainair, New Mexico, USA. The HAPS traveled southward a 2D distance of 105.3 km, with a 3D distance of 119.2 km. The HAPS reached a peak altitude of 25.3 km (83,000+ ft) above ground. The design of the high altitude RF data collection platform is described. Received LEO signals are modeled and software-defined receivers (SDRs) are implemented to estimate the Doppler frequency shift from downlink signals. Extensive experimental results are presented analyzing Starlink and OneWeb downlink signals over four HAPS segments: (i) stationary at ground-level, (ii) initial takeoff, (iii) cruising altitude, and (iv) peak altitude. SDR tracking results are presented comparing the performance during stationary and in-flight scenarios, and the the received carrier-to-noise density ratio (C/N0) is characterized. An extended Kalman filter (EKF) is designed to fuse Starlink and OneWeb Doppler measurements with altimeter data to estimate the HAPS' states, assuming two dynamical models: velocity random walk and Wiener process acceleration, with the latter model yielding more accurate results, namely a 3D position root mean-squared errors (RMSEs) of (i) 306 m over 4.12 km during initial ascent, (ii) 729 m over 28.24 km while traveling through a presumed jet stream, and (iii) as accurate as 17 m at peak altitude over a 1.38 km trajectory.

title: Coupled Half-Bridge LLC Resonant Converter With Reduced Switches and Current Sharing Ability
authors: Xinhui Zhao, Hongliang Wang, Zongheng Wu, Lingling Zhang, Mingyang Han, Yan-Fei Liu, Xiumei Yue
abstract: A novel coupled half-bridge LLC resonant converter is proposed in this paper, which has fewer switches and automatic current sharing ability compared with the traditional parallel half-bridge LLC resonant converter. Since the two resonant networks in the proposed converter share the same power switches, the number of devices is reduced by half, which is beneficial for increasing system power density. In addition, the resonant inductor is shared by the two resonant networks, which means that the uneven current in the two LLC resonant converters, caused by differences in resonant parameters, can be shared. The operation mode of the proposed converter is analyzed, and it has the same characteristics as the traditional LLC resonant converter. Besides, the coupled resonant network is analyzed on the basis of Fundamental Harmonic Analysis (FHA), and the current sharing effect of the proposed converter is analyzed. Further, a 1kW experimental platform is built, and the theoretical analysis and simulation results are validated by experimental results.

title: Wholesale Market Participation via Competitive DER Aggregation
authors: Cong Chen, Ahmed S. Alahmed, Timothy D. Mount, Lang Tong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: We consider the aggregation of distributed energy resources (DERs), such as solar PV, energy storage, and flexible loads, by a profit-seeking aggregator participating directly in the wholesale market under distribution network access constraints. We propose a competitive DER aggregator (DERA) model that directly controls local DERs to maximize its profits, while ensuring each aggregated customer gains a surplus higher than their surplus under the regulated retail tariff. The DERA participates in the wholesale electricity market as virtual storage with optimized generation offers and consumption bids derived from the proposed competitive aggregation model. Also derived are DERA's bid curves for the distribution network access and DERA's profitability when competing with the regulated retail tariff. We show that, with the same distribution network access, the proposed DERA's wholesale market participation achieves the same welfare-maximizing outcome as when its customers participate directly in the wholesale market. Extensive numerical studies compare the proposed DERA with existing methods in terms of customer surplus and DERA profit. We empirically evaluate how many DERAs can survive in the competition at the long-run equilibrium, and assess the impacts of DER adoption levels and distribution network access on short-run operations.

title: Congruent kinesthetic and tactile haptic rendering accelerates the learning of highly dynamic virtual sensorimotor tasks_supp2-3698607.mp4
authors: L. ALEXANDRE RATSCHAT

title: FedTransformer-Edge: A Unified Framework for Resource-Adaptive Federated Transformer Learning with Cross-Modal Attention in Heterogeneous IIoT Networks
authors: Iftikhar Rasheed, Hala Mostafa, Muhammad Adeel, Saad Alahmari, Saad Nasser AlTamimi
abstract: Deploying transformer-based anomaly detection in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) networks poses unique challenges including stringent latency requirements, heterogeneous protocols, and safety-critical operations. The proposed work combines multimodal federated learning with adaptive resource optimization, specifically designed for diverse edge devices by addressing three key challenges: (1) a Cross-Modal Progressive Attention (CMPA) mechanism that enables the efficient fusion of vibration, temperature, and pressure sensor data through trainable routing matrices, thus, reducing the complexity from O(T2max) to O(Tmax log Tmax); (2) a Resource-Adaptive Federated Optimization (RAFO) algorithm with provable convergence guarantees under non-IID and asynchronous conditions; and (3) an adaptive differential privacy scheme that dynamically adjusts privacy budgets based on model convergence and data sensitivity. The theoretical analysis confirms that the convergence bounds under asynchronous updates and privacy composition is guaranteed. Evaluation is performed on four IIoT datasets (TON-IoT, SWAT, WADI, and HAI) that demonstrates that FedTransformer-Edge attains 91.3% F1-score (±2.1%, 95% CI) while reducing communication overhead by 62% and energy consumption by 48% compared to other state-of-the-art base-lines. The framework maintains real-time performance (3.7ms average latency) on resource-constrained devices with 512MB RAM, validated through deployment on 50 heterogeneous edge devices.

title: Modeling, Simulation, and Prototype Fabrication of a Saturable-Core Reactor
authors: Ábner C. P. Pacheco, Ivan N. Santos, Elise Saraiva, Guilherme D. Da C. Oliveira, Paulo H. O. Rezende, Gustavo T. A. Da Silva, José A. De S. Brito
abstract: This paper presents the complete development of a 500 kVAr saturable-core reactor prototype, including design, computational modeling, fabrication and laboratory testing. The design was based on classical electromagnetic‐circuit principles, and the resulting data were used by TRAEL Transformers to manufacture the equipment and were implemented computationally in ANSYS. Laboratory tests were conducted to assess its operation and effectiveness; among these, the power–voltage characteristic was used to compare experimental results with those from simulation. The analysis demonstrates that the design exhibits robust performance for power-system applications.

title: Pretrained Model Recommendation for Downstream Fine-tuning
authors: Jiameng Bai, Sai Wu, Jie Song, Junbo Zhao, Gang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: As a fundamental problem in transfer learning, model selection aims to rank off-the-shelf pretrained models and select the most suitable one for the new target task. Existing model selection techniques are often constrained in their scope and tend to overlook the nuanced relationships between models and tasks. In this paper, we present a pragmatic framework Fennec, delving into a diverse, large-scale model repository while meticulously considering the intricate connections between tasks and models. The key insight is to map all models and historical tasks into a transfer-related subspace, where the distance between model vectors and task vectors represents the magnitude of transferability. A large vision model, as a proxy, infers a new task's representation in the transfer space, thereby circumventing the computational burden of extensive forward passes and reliance on labels. We also investigate the impact of the inherent inductive bias of models on transfer results and propose a novel method called archi2vec to encode the intricate structures of models. The transfer score is computed through straightforward vector arithmetic with a constant time complexity of O(k) per model inference (where k is the dimension of the latent space). Finally, we make a substantial contribution to the field by releasing a comprehensive benchmark that includes 105 different models. We validate the effectiveness of Fennec through rigorous testing on two benchmarks. Our framework achieves the best evaluation accuracy on both benchmarks. On the PARC benchmark, the feature extraction time is 6.5× faster than the baseline at the same level, and the inference time is 5.7× faster than the fastest baseline. On the larger benchmark, the performance gains are even more pronounced, with feature extraction time being 52.3× faster and inference time 1.8× faster than the fastest baseline, demonstrating remarkable performance efficiency. The code has been made publicly available at: https://github.com/Fay-why/Fennec.

title: A Soft Worm Robot for Sub-Centimeter Scale Pipeline Inspection_supp1-3699247.mp4
authors: Yitong Zhou
abstract: Inspection of sub-centimeter pipelines with continuous spatial curvatures remains challenging due to extreme geo metric confinement. This paper presents an ultralight, trunkless worm-inspired robot actuated by a single twisted and coiled actuator (TCA) specifically optimized for short-range inspection within localized extreme spatial bottlenecks. By integrating actuation and structural support into a single TCA and eliminating rigid trunks and auxiliary recovery mechanisms, the robot achieves a compact and lightweight design, with a mass of 0.5–1.1 g, a length of 32 mm, and a diameter of 4–10 mm. Directional peristaltic locomotion is enabled by a wedge-based micro silicone frustum foot that provides passive frictional anisotropy without complex control. A simplified mechanics-based anchoring model captures the dominant directional friction asymmetry induced by foot geometry and guides parameter optimization. Experiments demonstrate a maximum friction anisotropy ratio of 4.75, enabling stable crawling at 0.40 mm/s horizontally and 0.13 mm/s vertically in 4–9 mm pipelines, including complex three dimensional curved configurations. Blind inspection experiments with an onboard micro-camera further validate the robot's potential for inspection in highly confined pipeline environments.

title: Robust Rigid Body Pose Estimation for Sparse Point Sets Under Heteroscedastic Noise
authors: Hao Wang, Taogang Hou, Tianmiao Wang, Buhui Jiang
abstract: Rigid body pose estimation from sparse point correspondences is important in robotics but remains challenging when measurements are affected by heteroscedastic noise and occasional outliers. This issue is particularly evident in marker-based tracking of fast-moving platforms, where localization uncertainty may vary substantially across markers due to motion blur, occlusion, and sensing artifacts. To address this setting, we develop a robust registration framework tailored to sparse and structured point sets by modeling residuals with a heavy-tailed Student's t-distribution. Using its Gaussian scale mixture representation, the estimation problem is formulated as a maximum-likelihood objective with latent correspondence reliabilities, and solved via an iteratively reweighted least-squares procedure with a graduated non-convexity schedule. The resulting method adaptively down-weights unreliable measurements while retaining the efficiency of weighted rigid alignment. Simulation results across multiple noise models and outlier ratios show that the proposed approach achieves consistently low rotation and translation errors in sparse regimes. Real-world experiments on high-speed quadrotor tracking further demonstrate improved geometric consistency and reduced trajectory jerk relative to standard least-squares estimation.

title: Reconstructing Temporal Heterogeneity: A Multi-domain Collaborative Analysis Framework for Robust Time-Series Forecasting
authors: Hengrui Li, Wenxue Cui, Yifeng Wang, Chunshan Dong, Wenju Li, Tianyi Lu, Jiangpeng Shi, Jianfeng Wang, Yongbing Zhang, Shaohui Liu
abstract: Real-world time series data typically exhibit multi-scale patterns, heterogeneous structures, and non-stationarity. Capturing these multifaceted temporal variations remains a fundamental yet challenging problem in time-series forecasting. Despite considerable advancements in recent years, most existing approaches still depend on conventional decomposition paradigms that separate time series into trend and seasonal components, while neglecting abrupt events, and systemic perturbations in practical applications. This limitation not only introduces forecasting bias but also hinders model generalization and interpretability in complex environments. To tackle these difficulties, we propose a Multi-domain Collaborative Analysis Framework (MCAF). The framework addresses temporal heterogeneity through structural decoupling and domain-specific modeling. Specifically, each time series is decomposed into three distinct components: trend, seasonal, and residual. These components are then processed through specialized modeling pathways, each tailored to capture their unique dynamics in either the time, frequency, or time-frequency domain. A unified fusion mechanism subsequently integrates the extracted multi-domain representations. Extensive experiments show that MCAF consistently delivers high accuracy and robustness across diverse forecasting horizons.

title: An Actively Controlled Quasi-Resonant Excitation Topology for High-Resolution Transient Electromagnetic Sensors
authors: Tao Guo, Zhenzhu Xi, He Wang, Sheng Zhou, Yinlong Xu, Jiaming Sun, Wei Liu
abstract: The transient electromagnetic (TEM) method relies on acquiring weak secondary magnetic fields for subsurface sensing. However, the highly inductive nature of the transmitting sensor coil inherently prevents an instantaneous turn-off of the excitation current. This non-ideal trailing effect generates a primary field interference that heavily contaminates early-time secondary signals, creating a severe shallow detection blind zone. Even when spatial coil topologies are employed to offset the primary field, early-time resolution remains strictly constrained by the transient performance of the current falling edge. To address this sensor-level bottleneck, this article proposes a novel quasi-resonant fast turn-off circuit designed to optimize the excitation waveform for TEM systems. By integrating a series resonant capacitor and an active damping network, the proposed topology achieves rapid current transition without requiring bulky energy storage, thereby preserving instrument portability. Experimental and simulation results indicate that the circuit effectively completes the turn-off process of an 8.8 A excitation current within 20 μs while maintaining excellent linearity. Furthermore, breaking through the constraints of passive snubber circuits, the turn-off duration can be flexibly extended via active control logic to meet specific shallow-detection requirements. Comparative analysis with an energy-dissipation quasi-resonant rectifying circuit (EDQRRC) demonstrates that the proposed method significantly shortens the turn-off duration and suppresses post-turn-off parasitic oscillations. This hardware advancement effectively minimizes the shallow detection blind zone, providing robust support for the acquisition of high-fidelity early-time secondary field data.

title: CuDi: Curve Distillation for Efficient and Controllable Exposure Adjustment
authors: Chongyi Li, Chunle Guo, Ruicheng Feng, Shangchen Zhou, Chen Change Loy
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: We present Curve Distillation, CuDi, for efficient and controllable exposure adjustment without the requirement of paired or unpaired data during training. Our method inherits the zero-reference learning and curve-based framework from an effective low-light image enhancement method, Zero-DCE, with further speed up in its inference speed, reduction in its model size, and extension to controllable exposure adjustment. The improved inference speed and lightweight model are achieved through novel curve distillation that approximates the time-consuming iterative operation in the conventional curve-based framework by high-order curve's tangent line. The controllable exposure adjustment is made possible with a new self-supervised spatial exposure control loss that constrains the exposure levels of different spatial regions of the output to be close to the brightness distribution of an exposure map serving as an input condition. Different from most existing methods that can only correct either underexposed or overexposed photos, our approach corrects both underexposed and overexposed photos with a single model. Notably, our approach can additionally adjust the exposure levels of a photo globally or locally with the guidance of an input condition exposure map, which can be pre-defined or manually set in the inference stage. Through extensive experiments, we show that our method is appealing for its fast, robust, and flexible performance, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in real scenes. Project page: https://li-chongyi.github.io/CuDi_files/.

title: Variational Enhancement of Coded Fringes via Bi-directional Coded Content Similarity for Structured-light 3D Measurement
authors: Wei Cao, Xiaodong Na, Tianzhe Bao, Zhan Song, Shengquan Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In structured-light (SL) 3D measurement systems, underexposed coded fringe images (UCFIs) often fail to decode and reconstruct sparse point clouds due to low visibility. Unfortunately, existing low-light enhancement algorithms primarily target natural non-coded images, cannot faithfully preserve the encoded information of the fringe patterns, and are prone to introducing undesirable visual artifacts, noise, and reconstruction distortion. To this end, we first propose a novel bidirectional coded content similarity (B2CS) rule, specifically designed to enhance UCFIs captured by SL systems. Based on this rule, it reveals that the failure reason of existing methods occurs by breaking coded content similarity between under- and well-exposed fringes, and accordingly we construct a hybrid L2-L1-L0 norm-constrained decomposition model with illumination-reflectance-noise priors. Considering the non-convexity caused by the joint priors, we design an effective variational decomposition framework and employ the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) for efficient optimization. Based on the reliable decomposed solution, the enhanced UCFIs can assist the structured light algorithm in recovering structurally rich and high-density point clouds. Extensive experiments on real-world SL datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both subjective and objective evaluation, and significantly improves the completeness of the 3D reconstructed point cloud (by ≥ 20 %).

title: ACT-Net: Adaptive Compensation Transformer Network for Real-Time Detection in Remote Sensing Multimodal Imagery
authors: Hengyu Guo, Shiyu Yang, Qunyong Wu, Zhanchao Huang, Mengmeng Li, Yuhang Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Real-time RGB-Thermal (RGB-T) object detection using embedded remote sensing devices, such as Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), has become a research hotspot in the fields of remote sensing image intelligent processing and computer vision. However, the imaging characteristics of multimodal data present the core challenge of how to effectively fuse multimodal information while maintaining a lightweight model, without overshadowing the advantages of individual modalities. To address these issues, we propose an efficient embedded detection network, ACT-Net. Firstly, a pixel-level fusion strategy, Embedded Pixel-level Multimodal Fusion (EPMF), is utilized to accommodate the multimodal information fusion requirements of various embedded devices. Then, we introduce a Visual Feature Extraction Block (VFE-Block) that explicitly aggregates local, surrounding, and global context. This design increases the effective receptive field while preserving edges at low computational cost, enabling robust feature extraction for multimodal inputs. Furthermore, we propose an Adaptive Compensation Module (ACM) based on pixel-level fusion, which combines the lightweight advantage of pixel-level fusion with the precision performance of feature-level fusion. Lastly, we introduce the Partition Spatial Attention (PSA), which effectively addresses the problem of edge information loss during feature processing. Experimental results on the DroneVehicle and M3FD multimodal datasets show that, compared to RT-DETR-r18, ACT-Net achieves improvements of 2.0% and 3.8% in mAP, respectively. Furthermore, the model reduces the number of parameters by 38.4%, and achieves a detection speed of 66 FPS on the Jetson AGX Xavier under TensorRT-accelerated FP32 end-to-end deployment, thus meeting the real-time requirements of RGB-T object detection. The source codes are available at: https://github.com/AlanGwok/ACT-Net

title: A 144- μW Phase-Interval Balanced RVCO With 142% Tuning Range and 218.2-dBc/Hz FoM TA
authors: Haochuan Wang, Tingwei Gong, Chenglong Zhu, Wenkai Jin, Guodong Su, Mang I. Vai, Sio Hang Pun, Yuhua Cheng, Guo Qing Luo, Hui Hong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This paper presents a phase-interval balanced ring voltage-controlled oscillator (RVCO) with ultra-low power and ultra-wide frequency tuning range (FTR), for wearable or implanted brain-computer sensors. An approximate phase-interval error analysis model is developed to quantify the phase imbalance introduced by switched capacitors in phase-combined RVCOs. Based on this model, digitally controlled switched capacitors are incorporated into the low-power RVCO core to significantly expand the FTR while reducing phase-interval imbalance. An enhanced time-window phase combiner (E-TWPC) is further proposed to improve tolerance to residual phase-interval errors and mitigate phase noise degradation over a wide operating range. Prototyped in 65-nm CMOS, the proposed oscillator consumes only 16-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$144~\mu{W}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and achieves an FTR of 440MHz-2.6GHz (142%) under 4-bit digital control. This RVCO maintains comparable phase noise performance to existing RVCOs, while occupying a compact area of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.00057~mm^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, achieving a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathrm{FoM}_{TA}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> of 218.2dBc/Hz at 1MHz offset.

title: Distributed Fixed-Time Control With Adaptive Barrier Function in AC Microgrid Under Dynamic Event-Triggered Mechanism
authors: Chanjuan Zhao, Yue Li, Wei Sun, Qingwei Tang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a novel dynamic event-triggered (DET) fixed-time control method to solve the practical voltage and frequency consensus problem in the islanded microgrid. By introducing a new dynamic variable with multi-scale negative self-feedback terms rather than a constant, a more serious and time-variant triggering condition is proposed to reduce triggering instants and controller updates. Additionally, an adaptive barrier function and a disturbance observation-like method are integrated into the fixed-time control to enhance precise convergence control. Furthermore, the microgrid fixed-time consensus analysis and Zeno behavior exclusion are verified by the Lyapunov stability theorem. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate the feasibility of our proposed control.

title: IEEE Foundation
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Aerospace and Electronic Systems Magazine

title: Hierarchical Multi-Terminal DC System for Large-Scale Renewable Integration: Topology and Coordinated Control
authors: Tian Hou, Ying Huang, Guoteng Wang
abstract: As DC power projects transition toward phased construction and higher voltage levels, interconnecting existing systems to form a multi-voltage-level DC grid is a pragmatic choice that balances the wide-area integration of large-scale renewable energy with the maximization of legacy asset utilization. While DC/DC transformer (DCT) topologies provide viable technical paths, scaling them to ultra-high-voltage applications involves considerations such as full-scale hardware requirements and isolation transformer design. To facilitate more flexible phased expansion, this paper proposes a Hierarchical Multi-Terminal DC (HMTDC) system with Coordinated Primary and Secondary Control (CPSC). The topology employs Multi-Voltage-Level Interconnecting MMCs (MVLI-MMCs) rated for the inter-system voltage differential rather than full terminal voltage, enabling gigawatt-level power exchange through significantly reduced converter capacity. The CPSC combines distributed droop-based primary control for rapid disturbance rejection with secondary optimization that coordinates power margins and voltage quality across subsystems. Comprehensive time-domain simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework not only optimizes cross-level power flows but also ensures fault ride-through and rapid recovery under severe AC/DC contingencies, thereby providing a techno-economically viable pathway for evolving multi-voltage-level DC grids.

title: Passivity-Based Pinning Synchronization Control of Switched Directed Networks With Improved Switching Strategy
authors: Chongyi Cui, Hong Sang, Yi Liu, Peng Wang, Shuanghe Yu, Georgi Marko Dimirovski
abstract: This research delves into the passivity-based pinning synchronization problem for switched directed networks (SDNs) within the context of a sampled-data-based event-triggered (SDET) communication environment. An improved state-dependent switching strategy (SDSS) using sampled measurements alongside a novel time-dependent Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional (LKF) approach is constructed. Subsequently, a relaxed passivity analysis framework is then presented, ensuring that the resultant synchronization error system (SES) remains passive, and that the examined SDNs achieve asymptotic synchronization in the absence of external inputs. Unlike conventional strategies, the proposed switching protocol depends exclusively on system states at discrete sampling instants and effectively utilizes historical state data, yielding a less conservative theoretical framework for passivity synthesis in SDNs. Furthermore, the resulting synchronization criterion is independent of the number of network nodes, rendering it suitable for SDNs with large size. Ultimately, two examples involving relevant application in image encryption and decryption are provided to verify the effectiveness and applicability of the proposed scheme, and highlight the distinct advantage of switching behaviors in enhancing encryption confidentiality.

title: Pulse Repetition Frequency Tuned Low-Intensity Focused Ultrasound Neuromodulation for Tibial Nerve Targeted Bladder Function Modulation
authors: Young-Soo Lim, Hyunhee Kim, Chulhong Kim, Sung-Min Park
abstract: Objective: This study aims to investigate whether the neuromodulatory efficacy of low-intensity focused ultrasound (LIFU) for peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) can be systematically tuned by the temporal stimulation parameter, pulse repetition frequency (PRF). Methods: LIFU-mediated tibial nerve stimulation (LIFU-TNS) was applied with multiple PRF conditions to evaluate PRF-dependent neuromodulatory effects, with micturition inhibition as the primary outcome. Intra-bladder pressure (IBP) was continuously monitored to evaluate changes in micturition intervals. Neural involvement was confirmed using (1) a lidocaine-induced sciatic nerve (tibial branch) block, and the stimulation paradigm was additionally evaluated in (2) an acetic acid–induced overactive bladder model and (3) freely moving, unconstrained animals to assess robustness across physiological and behavioral conditions. Results: A distinct PRF-dependent neuromodulation was observed, where 100 Hz stimulation elicited the most potent inhibition of micturition, whereas 10 Hz and 1 kHz showed comparatively less effective results. Quantitative analysis of normalized micturition interval (post-stimulation / pre stimulation baseline) confirmed this trend. (10 Hz: 0.98±0.16; 100 Hz: 1.72±0.81; 1 kHz: 0.95±0.35). Importantly, consistent inhibition was replicated across the nerve-block, overactive bladder, and freely moving models, indicating robustness of the effect under diverse physiological and behavioral conditions. Conclusion: The clear PRF-dependent modulation observed in LIFU-TNS establishes PRF as a feasible and tunable control parameter and highlights its potential for guiding precise neuromodulation. Significance: Unlike conventional intensity dependent strategies, this study demonstrates that PRF is essential for effective ultrasound neuromodulation in overactive bladder. These findings establish a systematic parameter-based framework for developing robust peripheral nerve interfaces.

title: LCSM: A Local Chart-based Statistical Model for Femoral Shape Analysis
authors: Lujian Zhang, Tianyu Fu, Dejin Yang, Jingyi Liu, Jiaju Zhang, Deqiang Xiao, Jingfan Fan, Danni Ai, Hong Song, Yixin Zhou, Jian Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Statistical shape modeling of the femur is vital for clinical applications, yet linear methods struggle with complex geometric variations. While recent chart-based generative models like ChartPointFlow excel at general point cloud reconstruction, their direct application to the nuanced domain of SSM is limited. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel framework that adapts and significantly extends the chart-based paradigm for robust statistical shape analysis. Our core contributions are threefold. First, we are applying this substructure-based generative approach to the statistical modeling of anatomical shapes, moving beyond reconstruction. Second, to overcome the limitations of baseline encoders in capturing intricate anatomical details, we design a Geometric-enhanced Dynamic Graph Convolutional encoder that integrates multi-level geometric feature encoding. Third, to better learn the statistical distribution across a population, we introduce a point-wise feature augmentation module, termed Fine Feature Mixture module, which enriches feature diversity and robustness by fusing fine-grained representations across different samples. Extensive reconstruction and generation experiments on a femur dataset demonstrate our framework’s superiority. Furthermore, evaluations on multiple ShapeNet categories validate its generalization and applicability to diverse 3D shape modeling tasks. Code will be available in https://github.com/LujZhang/LCSM.

title: The Design and Fabrication of a Compact Band-Stop Filter Based on Bulk Acoustic Resonator
authors: Yuannian Li, Weipeng Xuan, Hong Jiang, Rui Ding, Long Chen, Peng Zheng, Wenjun Li, Lingling Sun, Hao Jin, Jikui Luo, Shurong Dong
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this work, a compact bulk acoustic wave (BAW) resonator–based band-stop filter operating at 2 GHz was designed, fabricated, and characterized. The Mason model of the bulk acoustic resonator is used to design the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pi $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-topology configuration filter. To optimize filter performance and reduce area mismatch between series and shunt resonators, the series resonator is equivalently divided into two parallel resonators, effectively minimizing the area difference among the resonators that compose of the filter. The fabricated ladder-type filter achieves a center frequency of 2057 MHz with a 3 dB stopband width of 34 MHz and up to 20 dB rejection with only a 9 MHz sideband. The filter shows a passband insertion loss (IL) below 1.2 dB, with a minimum IL of 0.7 dB and band-stop rejection of more than 15 dB. Power-handling tests show that the filter can withstand up to 24 dBm of continuous sine-wave input power. The experimental and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed BAW-based stopband filter achieves low IL, high rejection, and excellent power endurance, providing a promising candidate for integration into next-generation RF front-end modules and compact high-frequency communication systems.

title: Secure Edge–Fog Architecture for Social IoT and Opportunistic Network Applications
authors: Nidhi Mishra, Utkarsh Anand
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: While existing research often surveys edge-fog computing in static or infrastructure-dependent contexts, this study addresses a critical gap by designing and testing a novel, unified secure edge-fog architecture specifically tailored for Social Internet of Things (SIoT) and opportunistic network services. Working in resource-limited and intermittently connected environments, the proposed framework is unique in that it combines social awareness with decentralized security to reduce the use of centralized cloud infrastructure. It designed a multi-layer architecture, which involved socially related IoT devices, local processing nodes (edge nodes), and regional coordination nodes (fog nodes). Its most important contribution is a model of composite trust evaluation and a dynamic social graph that, when combined, make it possible to communicate safely via store-carry-forward capabilities, without any prior trust. In order to have the highest performance, the architecture will enable adaptive computation offloading and privacy-preserving preprocessing of data. The simulation outcomes with different node movement and attack conditions show that there are great improvements compared to cloud-centric architectures and non-social ones. Namely, the proposed system reported an average communication latency of 72 ms (as compared to 185 ms in cloud-centric models) and minimized the energy usage to 53 J as compared to 92 J. Moreover, the packet delivery ratio rose with 94% out of 78% respectively in comparison with cloud-centric systems, whereas the decentralized trust system made it very stable and rapid convergence to isolate malicious nodes. These results are a confirmation that social consciousness and decentralized trust embedded within an edge-fog architecture are an effective solution offering resilience and high performance in real-world use in dynamically changing, infrastructure-constrained SIoT environments.

title: Unveiling the Drivers of E-Grocery Usage Behaviour in Urban Areas
authors: Yuka Maheswara, Yulia Magdalena, Yuli Eni
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: E-grocery has recently expanded in Indonesia because of Covid-19. However, the adoption rate of E-grocery is considerably lower than other e-commerce platforms. In order to investigate this, this study used UTAUT to find what influences the adoption rate of the consumers towards $\mathbf{E}$ Grocery. 103 respondents completed a questionnaire. The results show that the main factors influencing the rate of e-grocery adoption are Social Influence and Facilitating Conditions. While Facilitating Conditions have a significant impact on Use Behaviour, Social Influence has a significant impact on Behavioural Intention. It was discovered that neither Performance Expectancy nor Effort Expectancy significantly affects Behavioural Intention. These findings show that social factors and technological support have a greater influence on e-grocery adoption in Indonesian cities than perceived effectiveness and ease of use.

title: Beamforming Enhancement Using Unipolar Directivity of Pressure-Gradient Vector Hydrophone
authors: Zan Li, Xinjing Huang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: Beamforming with scalar hydrophone linear arrays suffers from port-starboard ambiguity, and grating lobes emerge when the element spacing exceeds half a wavelength, which impairs the detection and localization of acoustic targets. A beamforming enhancement method is proposed based on the unipolar directivity of pressure-gradient vector hydrophones, which can suppress acoustic source interference from undesired directions and eliminate port-starboard ambiguity. The receiving model of a two-dimensional pressure-gradient vector hydrophone is established, and two derived quantities with unipolar directivities are constructed using only the sound pressure collected by each channel. By analyzing the receiving theory of linear arrays, it is demonstrated that the unipolar directivity can effectively suppress grating lobes and enable selective reception of targets. A microphone cluster is used to simulate a pressuregradient vector hydrophone in air, to test its directivity and suppression effect on interference (grating lobes) from undesired directions during beamforming. The experimental results show that the directivities of the derived quantities are consistent with the theoretical results, and the main lobe intensities of incident waves from $70^{\circ}, 47^{\circ}, 33^{\circ}$ and 21° are suppressed to $48.3 \%, 9.3 \%$, 6.2% and 2.6% of that of the incident wave from 93°, respectively. This method provides a solution for the anti-interference detection and localization of acoustic sources with large apertures and short wavelengths.

title: Random Forest based Predictive Vehicle Breakdown Management System for Intelligent Roadside Assistance
authors: G.Mariammal, Gnana Moorthi P, Jona Jebarson J, Dhayaa Nidhi A, Arulkumaar K
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Unexpected vehicle breakdowns remain a significant concern in modern transportation environments due to increasing vehicle density, complex mechanical systems, and unpredictable operational conditions. Roadside failures often result in traffic congestion, safety risks, delayed emergency response, and financial losses associated with repair and towing services. Conventional vehicle maintenance strategies rely on periodic servicing schedules and manual inspection practices that fail to reflect real-time operational conditions of vehicle components. Such reactive approaches frequently allow hidden mechanical issues to progress until sudden failure occurs during travel. The proposed work introduces an Intelligent Real-Time Roadside Vehicle Assistance and Predictive Breakdown Management System designed to identify potential vehicle failure risks before critical breakdown events occur. The system integrates vehicle operational parameters, historical maintenance information, and performance indicators within a unified predictive framework. Collected vehicle data undergo systematic preprocessing to remove inconsistencies and to prepare structured feature sets representing mechanical health conditions. A machine learning based predictive model analyzes operational attributes including engine temperature, engine speed, mileage patterns, oil pressure levels, battery health status, fuel consumption behavior, and vehicle service history. Prediction outputs are converted into a dynamic risk score that reflects the likelihood of mechanical failure. The framework also provides a web-based monitoring interface for real-time vehicle health assessment, historical risk analysis, and preventive maintenance guidance. Automated alert mechanisms activate when risk levels exceed safety thresholds, allowing early intervention before roadside failure occurs. The system additionally supports route safety evaluation and maintenance recommendations to enhance travel reliability and reduce unexpected disruptions. Overall evaluation demonstrates high predictive accuracy and reliable detection capability for potential vehicle failures. The proposed system contributes to improved transportation safety, reduced maintenance costs, and proactive vehicle health management through data-driven predictive analytics.

title: LLM-Assisted Carbon-Aware Coordinated EV Charging for Decarbonization
authors: Chengdong Yang
publish: 2026 3rd International Conference on Power System Engineering and Smart Grid (PSESG)
abstract: Against the backdrop of decarbonization and rapid transportation electrification, large-scale electric vehicle (EV) charging introduces both opportunities and challenges for power-system operation. On the one hand, flexible EV charging can help absorb low-carbon electricity and improve renewable-energy utilization; on the other hand, growing charging demand increases scheduling complexity under time-varying grid conditions and diverse user requirements. Conventional charging strategies are typically based on price signals, load shifting, or predefined optimization rules, but they often underuse carbon-intensity information and have limited ability to incorporate user preferences expressed in natural language. To address these issues, this paper proposes an LLM-assisted carbon-aware coordinated EV charging framework. The proposed method combines time-varying carbon intensity, electricity prices, load information, and user-side constraints within a hierarchical architecture, where a large language model (LLM) provides high-level scheduling recommendations and a lower-level optimizer minimizes carbon cost and charging smoothness under operational constraints. A case study based on real-world EV traces and CAISO carbon-intensity data compares the proposed method with four benchmark strategies: Uncontrolled, Valley Filling, Price Based, and Carbon Aware. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves the lowest average carbon emissions, reducing daily emissions from 71.55 kg in the Uncontrolled baseline to 56.13 kg, while maintaining competitive charging cost and near-complete target-SOC satisfaction. In addition, paired statistical tests confirm that the carbon-reduction improvement is significant. These results demonstrate that LLM-assisted coordinated charging provides a practical and effective approach for low-carbon EV integration.

title: From Static Surveys to Adaptive Questionnaires: A Systematic Review of Generative AI–Driven Personalization in Education
authors: Saif AbuAisheh, Ying Wah Teh, Liyana Shuib
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Computational Intelligence Approaches and Applications (ICCIAA)
abstract: Personalized assessment is a cornerstone of adaptive learning and learner analytics, yet traditional static surveys and questionnaires often fail to reflect learners’ unique needs or evolving understanding. Recent progress in generative artificial intelligence (AI), particularly large language models (LLMs) and natural-language processing systems, enables questionnaires to be generated, adapted, and scored dynamically. This systematic literature review synthesizes peer-reviewed research published between 2019 and 2025 on generative-AI-driven personalization in educational questionnaires. Following PRISMA-inspired procedures, we defined research questions, searched four digital libraries (IEEE Xplore, Scopus, Web of Science, and SpringerLink). The synthesis highlights how generative models support scalable item production, personalized question sequencing, and adaptive feedback across cognitive levels. At the same time, the literature reports recurring limitations including hallucinated or irrelevant items, bias and fairness risks, privacy concerns, and continued reliance on human supervision. Evidence on long-term learning outcomes and governance remains limited. We consolidate current applications, summarize benefits and challenges, and identify research gaps and future directions, including hybrid approaches that integrate structured knowledge (e.g., knowledge graphs) with generation, transparent and fair item-generation pipelines, and cross-disciplinary evaluation of adaptive questionnaires.

title: Extractive Summarization using TextRank with FastText Word Embedding and Cosine Similarity for News Summarization
authors: Subhan Subhan, Novita Ayu Indirawati, Yahya Nur Ifriza
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The rapid evolution of technology has fundamentally reshaped the landscape of digital news dissemination. However, the continuous surge in available data has precipitated a state of information overload, forcing readers to navigate vast quantities of content—a process that often undermines overall cognitive efficiency. Consequently, automated text summarization has emerged as a pivotal solution for optimizing the consumption of information. This research utilizes the TextRank algorithm, integrated with FastText word embeddings and cosine similarity, to transform textual input into vector representations. This methodology facilitates the ranking of sentences based on their thematic relevance, ensuring that the generated summaries remain both precise and concise. To validate the system’s efficacy, the ROUGE metric was employed to conduct a comparative analysis between the system-generated output and human reference summaries. Empirical results demonstrated high performance, with F-scores of 0.92, 0.87, and 0.92 for ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, and ROUGE-L, respectively. Optimal outcomes were achieved by configuring the system to a maximum of ten sentences and applying a similarity threshold of 0.4 to govern the connectivity within the sentence network. This specific calibration allowed for a sophisticated balance between brevity and comprehensiveness, yielding high-quality summaries that closely mirror the structural and substantive integrity of the reference texts.

title: Explainable Employee Attrition Prediction using PCA-Optimized Random Forest and SHAP
authors: T. Padma, A.S. Kanishik, S. Dinesh
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Employee attrition is one of the most serious challenges in the organization because of its recruitment cost, loss of talent, and reduction in productivity. In this research, the Employee Attrition Prediction System has been developed using the Random Forest Machine Learning Algorithm. In this work, the dimensional reduction of the dataset has been carried out using the PCA technique in order to make the system efficient. The system also uses the SHAP technique for the interpretation of the results using the Random Forest Algorithm. Based on the historical data of employees such as age, department, role, years of experience, salary, and performance, the system determines whether the employees will stay in the organization or will attrite. Data preprocessing for high-quality data includes feature encoding, management of missing values, and removal of unnecessary features. After that, EDA is carried out for the understanding of the data distribution, pattern, and trend. This helps in highlighting the key reasons for employee attrition and aids in decision-making by the management. In addition to performing exploratory data analysis to detect trends and factors impacting employee attrition, feature engineering and feature importance analysis will be conducted to identify the most significant factors leading to attrition, including overtime hours, average monthly earnings, and total years worked. To ensure predictive accuracy and reliability, a Random Forest Model was tuned using hyperparameter optimization techniques such as RandomizedSearchCV and GridSearchCV to achieve the optimal model.

title: Does Bullying Harm Academic Achievement? Causal Machine Learning Evidence from Moroccan Students
authors: Ibrahim Allali, Mohammed Bijou, Lahboub Zouiri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study examines the causal effect of bullying exposure on students’ mathematics performance in Morocco using PISA 2022 data and the Generalized Random Forest (GRF) framework in a causal forest setting. Mathematics achievement is measured by the standardized PISA math score, while bullying is captured by the continuous index BULLIED, constructed from nine victimization-frequency items and scaled using an IRTbased procedure. To address confounding, we condition on a rich set of student, school, and socioeconomic covariates. The estimated average treatment effect indicates that a one-standard deviation increase in the bullying index, as defined by PISA, leads-ceteris paribus and conditional on observed individual and school characteristics-to an average decline of about 2.6 points in mathematics scores, revealing a statistically significant negative impact. Beyond the mean effect, the distribution of conditional average treatment effects shows substantial heterogeneity, suggesting that some students face markedly larger academic penalties from bullying exposure than others. Robustness checks support the credibility of the estimates: trimming to strengthen common support yields a similar negative effect (ATE $=-2.74$, $\mathrm{SE}=0.56$), a placebo test based on randomizing the bullying index produces a near-zero and insignificant effect, and sensitivity analyses confirm stability across model configurations and sample fractions. Overall, the findings provide causal machine learning evidence that bullying is a meaningful and unevenly distributed barrier to academic performance in Morocco.

title: How Social Media Algorithms and Financial Influencer Reach Effect on Investment Decisions of Gen Z
authors: Dedrick Soesanto, Yohannes Kurniawan
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study analyzes the influence of financial influencers on Generation Z ‘s investment decisions by considering the role of financial literacy, consumer confidence, and perceived risk. Generation Z was chosen because of the high intensity of social media use and its tendency to use financial influencers as a source of investment information. The research uses a quantitative approach through an online questionnaire survey to Generation $\mathbf{Z}$ in Jakarta who follow financial influencers and have investment interests or experience. The data was analyzed using the Partial Least Squares-Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) method. The results of the study show that financial influencers have a positive and significant influence on Generation Z’s investment decisions. Consumer trust has been proven to mediate this relationship, while financial literacy plays a role as a moderation variable that can reduce less rational investment decision-making. On the other hand, perceived risk has a negative effect on investment decisions. These findings affirm the importance of credibility of financial information and improving financial literacy in the investment decision-making process of Generation $\mathbf{Z}$

title: Analysis of HEXAD player types and GUESS user experience in FPS game case studies: Valorant, Counter Strike 2, and Rainbow Six Siege
authors: Jevon Brian Adriel, Dyah Wahyu Sukmaningsih
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Research has proven that satisfaction can lead to player loyalty and their tendency to continue playing. However, there is a gap in understanding the relationship between player motivation to play and their preference in terms of satisfaction. Therefore this study focuses on how various kinds of player motivation can lead to their overall satisfaction in gaming. The research is using a quantitative survey method where more than 150 responders who are actively playing competitive First Person Shooter (FPS) games such as Valorant, Counter 2, and Rainbow Six Siege participated in the research. The study used the HEXAD model to identify each player's motivation and used the GUESS scale to measure their satisfaction with the system. The result of the study shows some significant relationships between player satisfaction and several types of player motivation which overall the study suggest that even though FPS players are mostly driven by rewards and ranking progression, their natural satisfaction on FPS games are influenced by social interactions between players.

title: A Multi-Dimensional Framework for Adaptive Scaffolding in Large Language Model
authors: Hala El Yabouri, Fatima-Zohra Hibbi, Otman Abdoun
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Adaptivity in systems based on large language Models (LLMs) is a relatively new direction in the research of intelligent tutoring systems (ITSs) and user modeling. This paper gives a detailed description of how adaptivity is realized in ITSs that integrate LLMs or LLMs themselves as pedagogical tools. The review aims to answer the question: What are the constitutive dimensions of adaptivity in dialogue-based scaffolding with Large Language Models, and how can these dimensions be formalized into a multi-dimensional framework to guide design and evaluation? We began by defining the concept of adaptivity based on comparison between the implementation of the rigid, rule-based architectures of traditional ITS and probabilistic, generative approaches capable of dynamic adaptation. The field of AIED currently lacks a theoretical framework for conceptualizing adaptivity in LLM-based systems. We address this gap by synthesizing findings from a systematic literature review and coupling them with inductive thematic analysis to develop a comprehensive multi-dimensional framework of adaptivity. We analyze and identify four constitutive dimensions through which LLM-based systems modify their behavior in response to learner needs: Cognitive Adaptivity (modulating informational content to match knowledge states), Strategic Adaptivity (adjusting pedagogical structure and timing of support), Affective Adaptivity (regulating emotional engagement through tone and persona), and Metacognitive Adaptivity (supporting selfregulation and reflective learning processes). All dimensions proposed in the analysis phase are grounded in learning sciences theory, particularly Vygotsky’s Zone of Proximal Development and Wood, Bruner, and Ross’s concept of scaffolding. We also discuss the implications of this shift, including the hallucination trade-off and ethical considerations surrounding overscaffolding. Providing a framework geared toward designers and researchers for designing pedagogically effective LLMbased tutoring systems that move beyond superficial conversational fluency toward strong educational impact.

title: Design of an Intelligent Information System for Spaceborne Remote Sensing Data Processing
authors: Tong Liu
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: The massive volume and diverse formats of spaceborne remote sensing data pose challenges for intelligent processing. This paper designs an intelligent information processing system tailored for spaceborne remote sensing data. It adopts a microservices architecture to construct the system framework, employs deep learning for multi-source data fusion and intelligent target recognition, and utilizes a distributed realtime processing engine to address large-scale data processing issues. Field validation in disaster prevention and mitigation, urban planning, and agricultural census applications demonstrates that this solution significantly enhances remote sensing data processing efficiency and recognition accuracy, offering a novel pathway for intelligent applications of space-based remote sensing data.

title: Evaluating the Generalization Ability of an Ensemble-Based Fake News Detection Model across Multiple Domains
authors: Jyoti, Yogesh Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: In order to be put in practice, systems should be able to generalize beyond their training datasets on which they have been trained. This paper investigates the cross-domain generalizability of an NDetect fake news detection ensemble stacking model. The framework integrates various machine learning classifiers in a meta-learning fashion, trained on the ISOT dataset with TF-IDF text representations. Under the premise of being able to evaluate the trained model directly without fine-tuning across four individual heterogeneous benchmark datasets LIAR, FakeNewsNet, CoAID, and PHEME for domain shift robustness. The experimental results demonstrate strong in-domain performance 0.89 on ISOT with consistent yet diminished performance across unseen domains, thereby underlining some benefits and limitations of ensemble learning over distributional shift. These findings underscore the importance of an explicit cross-dataset evaluation paradigm when assessing different classes of fake news detectors.

title: Multi-Path Parallel Quantum Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Han Qi, Xintong Huo, Abdullah Gani
