title: Fourier modal method with selective modal reduction for scalable analysis of three-dimensional extended-area-emitting photonic structures.
authors: Myeonggyu Choi, Jonghyun Lee, Hwi Kim
abstract: This paper presents a reformulation of the three-dimensional Fourier modal method (3D-FMM) incorporating selective modal dimension reduction (MDR) for the efficient simulation of complex photonic structures. By leveraging the observation that light extraction in self-emissive devices, such as organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs), is primarily governed by a limited subset of physically relevant modes, the proposed method selectively retains these modes while excluding highly evanescent ones. This approach effectively reduces the computational size of main matrix operations, including matrix inversions, without a significant loss of accuracy. Specifically, by utilizing boundary scattering matrices (S-matrices) and phase retardation operators, the framework decomposes standard 4L х 4L linear systems into half-dimensional operations to minimize peak memory consumption. The developed 2L FMM enables flexible, adaptive mode selection throughout the multi-block simulation. Validation results for a 3D OLED structure demonstrate an 25.6% reduction in peak memory usage and an 13.9% reduction in computation time compared to conventional formulations, while maintaining a minimal 0.23% difference in extracted power.

title: Research on an AI Enhanced Interactive Narrative Model for Digital Comics
authors: Yue Zhou, Qi Li, Tianyi Sun, Meidi Zhang
abstract: Digital comics have become an important form of human media interaction, yet most existing comic platforms still rely on static presentation and limited narrative adaptivity. To improve narrative modeling in digital comics, this study proposes an AI enhanced interactive narrative model that formulates comic understanding as a visual narrative continuation task. Given a sequence of context panels, the proposed method predicts the most coherent next panel by jointly modeling spatial semantics, temporal progression, and stylistic consistency across comic images. To this end, we develop a dual branch spatio temporal architecture combined with a structured optimization strategy to better capture ordered story evolution under diverse visual styles. Experiments conducted on the Visual Storytelling Dataset (VIST) and ComicsPAP demonstrate that the proposed model consistently outperforms conventional multimodal encoders and recent large multimodal models in Accuracy, Top 3 Accuracy, Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and NDCG@5. In particular, the method shows strong robustness on heavily stylized comic data while maintaining favorable computational efficiency. These results indicate that the proposed model provides an effective and scalable solution for AI enhanced narrative understanding in digital comics.

title: Learning Context-Aware Neural ODE Dynamics for Adaptive Robotic Control
authors: Shao-Yi Yu, Jen-Wei Wang, Maya Horii, Masayoshi Tomizuka, Vikas Garg
abstract: Robotic systems deployed in uncertain and dynamically changing environments often face variations in contact conditions, aerodynamic effects, and external disturbances that challenge reliable control. To remain effective under modelbased control, these systems require dynamics models that can adapt to such changes, especially when direct access to complete environmental information is limited. To enable adaptability and facilitate integration with model predictive control, we propose a context-aware dynamics model based on neural ordinary differential equations, which infers environmental factors from state-action histories using a two-phase training procedure. We validate the approach across diverse robotic platforms, including a quadrotor in simulation, as well as a Sphero BOLT robot and a Fanuc manipulator in real-world experiments. The results demonstrate that our method effectively adapts to temporally and spatially varying environmental changes across different tasks. Videos are available here, and the source code is available at our GitHub repository.

title: PanSR: An Object-Centric Mask Transformer for Maritime Panoptic Segmentation
authors: Lojze Žust, Matej Kristan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Environmental panoptic perception and obstacle detection are key components in enabling autonomy for autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs). Recent studies have demonstrated that general panoptic methods, currently dominated by mask transformer approaches, are unsuitable for maritime environments, as they perform poorly in crowded scenes and in scenes with a wide range of object scales, highlighting the need for domain-specific panoptic methods. In this work, we identify several fundamental shortcomings of these recent methods and rethink architecture design choices from the perspective of perception for autonomous navigation. We introduce PanSR, a new panoptic segmentation method with the following main contributions: 1) an object-centric proposal extraction module for better handling of object size variety, 2) an improved ground-truth matching scheme consistent with proposal extraction, and 3) object-centric mask prediction for better instance separation of similar objects. With these enhancements, PanSR effectively mitigates instance merging, enhances small-object detection and increases performance in crowded scenes, delivering a notable +3.4% PQ improvement over state-of-the-art on the challenging LaRS benchmark and +8.4% PQ on WaterScenes, while retaining competitive performance on the autonomous driving perception benchmark Cityscapes. The code and models are publicly available on GitHub.

title: Event-triggered optimal tracking control for nonlinear networked Markovian switching systems: Q -learning approach
authors: Haiyang Sun, Wenhai Qi, Ju H. Park, Zheng-Guang Wu
abstract: This paper proposes a fuzzy optimal tracking control scheme for networked nonlinear Markovian switching systems under an event-triggered mechanism. An augmented system is constructed by integrating system dynamics with the reference signal. Then, an actor-critic Q-learning method achieves adaptive control without requiring prior knowledge of system matrices or transition probabilities. An event-triggered mechanism is introduced to update control actions on demand, significantly reducing network communication load while preserving tracking performance. An online actor-critic neural network algorithm is designed. The critic network approximates the value function, and the actor network updates the control policy in real time, enabling collaborative optimal tracking control. Theoretical analysis shows that the resulting neural network-driven closed-loop system exhibits bounded weight estimation errors and is uniformly ultimately bounded. Simulations performed on a tunnel diode circuit model confirm the effectiveness and resource efficiency of the proposed method in a networked environment.

title: An Integrated 64-Channel Optical-Fiber-Split OPM-MCG System
authors: Shudong Lin, Yujian Ma, Ziqi Yuan, Yaxiang Wang, Yueyang Zhai, Junjian Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Magnetocardiography (MCG) offers a contactless and noninvasive approach to evaluating cardiac electrophysiological activity by measuring its associated magnetic fields. Recent advances in optically pumped magnetometers (OPMs) with femtotesla-level sensitivity have enabled the development of compact, high-density MCG systems suitable for clinical applications. In this work, we present a 64-channel integrated OPM-MCG system based on self-developed miniaturized single-beam magnetometers (3.8 cm³ volume, average sensitivity better than 10 fT/√Hz, and best sensitivity of 6 fT/√Hz in the 2–30 Hz band). A micro–nanofabricated planar lightwave circuit splitter distributes a single laser source into 64 stable, low-loss optical paths, achieving substantial reductions in system size and cost compared with conventional MCG/MEG platforms. The modular design allows for straightforward expansion to higher channel counts and sensor densities. Comprehensive evaluations of sensitivity, bandwidth, and measurement accuracy confirm the system’s high performance. In a magnetically shielded environment, the system successfully captured high-quality adult MCG signals, enabling detailed time-frequency analysis and precise cardiac magnetic field mapping. These results demonstrate the feasibility of large-scale OPM-based MCG for noninvasive cardiac functional assessment and underscore its potential as a platform for future cardiac diagnostic studies.

title: BiPneu: Design and Control of a Bipolar-Pressure Pneumatic System for Soft Robots_supp1-3693622.mp4
authors: Yu Mei

title: Super-Resolution ISAC Receivers: An MCMC-Based Gridless Sparse Bayesian Learning Approach
authors: Keying Zhu, Xingyu Zhou, Jie Yang, Le Liang, Shi Jin
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Signal Processing
abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is crucial for low-altitude wireless networks (LAWNs), where the safety-critical demand for high-accuracy sensing creates a trade-off between precision and complexity for conventional methods. To address this, we propose a novel gridless sparse Bayesian learning (SBL) framework for joint super-resolution multi-target detection and high-accuracy parameter estimation with manageable computational cost. Our model treats target parameters as continuous variables to bypass the grid limitations of conventional approaches. This SBL formulation, however, transforms the estimation task into a challenging high-dimensional inference problem, which we address by developing an enhanced gradient-based Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithm. Our method integrates mini-batch sampling and the Adam optimizer to ensure computational efficiency and rapid convergence. Finally, we validate the framework's robustness in strong clutter and provide a theoretical benchmark by deriving the corresponding Bayesian Cram´er-Rao bound. Simulation results demonstrate remarkable super-resolution capabilities, successfully resolving multiple targets separated by merely 50% of the Rayleigh limit in range, 17% in velocity, and 52% in angle. At a signal-to-noise ratio of 20 dB, the algorithm achieves a multi-target detection probability exceeding 90% while concurrently delivering ultra-high accuracy, with root mean square error of 0.07 m, 0.024 m/s, and 0.015° for range, velocity, and angle, respectively. This robust performance, demonstrated against strong clutter, showcases its suitability for practical ISAC-LAWNs applications.

title: A Novel Fractional-Order Second-Order Sliding Mode Control For Nonlinear Systems Under Matched and Mismatched Uncertainties
authors: Weiqi Zhang, Chuanyu Sun, Yanmin Wang, Hany M. Hasanien, Kai Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: To overcome traditional limitations in handling complex disturbances, a novel fractional-order second-order sliding mode control (FOSOSMC) is developed for a class of nonlinear systems in the presence of matched and mismatched uncertainties, ensuring the properties of stability and dynamic performance. The main novelties are highlighted as follows. Firstly, a structurally flexible continuous SOSMC manifold incorporating FO calculus is constructed by taking into account the design of a family of sliding variables, which ensures that the state trajectory can converge to the equilibrium point accurately in a finite time and address the chattering problem owing to its memory and hereditary properties, while uniformly compensating for mismatched uncertainties. Furthermore, the straightforward sliding manifold structure allows for diverse designs of control laws. For simple single-input single-output systems, their fast convergence and high precision under lumped uncertainties can be guaranteed by combining the FOSOSM manifold and a fast-terminal sliding mode-type reaching law. For complex multi-input multi-output systems, a state-dependent adaptive gain is fitted to the FOSOSM manifold to obtain stronger robustness without sacrificing control accuracy or complicating parameter tuning. The global finite-time stability of the closed-loop dynamics is demonstrated by means of Lyapunov functions. Finally, simulation and experimental results verify the excellent control performance of the proposed method for nonlinear systems with uncertainties.

title: Decentralized AI-Driven Cyber-Resilient Control for Real-Time Intrusion Detection and Mitigation in Inverter-Dominated Grids
authors: Uzair Asif, Harsha Vardhan Reddy Modugu, Hamza AL-Hallaj, Mohammad B. Shadmand
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: Distributed energy resources rely on secure and robust communication between hierarchical control layers to ensure stability, reliability, and optimal coordination. It makes the system vulnerable to cyberattacks, such as false data injection (FDI) and denial-of-service (DoS) attacks, especially on communication channels. To address this problem, this paper proposes an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven, decentralized, and resilient control framework for real-time detection and mitigation of potential cyberattacks. Specifically, this work presents a hybrid AI model comprising a convolutional neural network (CNN) and a transformer that estimates control signals locally based on time-series measurements of power, frequency, and the rate of change of frequency. Moreover, an autoencoder is employed to detect and differentiate the presence of an attack on the received and locally predicted control signals. If the attack is detected, the proposed framework substitutes the manipulated signals with attack-free values to mitigate its impact and ensure nominal operation of the power-electronics-dominated grid, thereby minimizing reliance on the communication channel. The developed cyber-resilient control framework is evaluated in a network of adaptive droop-controlled grid-forming inverters (GFMIs), where a supervisory controller sends updated droop coefficients to all GFMIs to enhance frequency response and improve power sharing. Multiple case studies are conducted on a modified IEEE 14-bus system and a small-scale hardware setup to validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed intrusion detection and mitigation scheme.

authors: Chetna Garg, Harsupreet Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: This work investigates a MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> junctionless symmetric split-gate FET label-free biosensor. The efficacy of the proposed biosensor has been assessed by performing rigorous device simulations and employing the NEGF framework which relies on the coupled solution of Schrodinger-Poisson’s equation and takes into account the quantum confinement effects emerging in the sub-10 nm regime. The sensing performance has been assessed by studying different biomolecules and it has been demonstrated that MoS<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> JLSymSG FET biosensor delivers the highest threshold voltage sensitivity, S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Vth</inf> = 384, drain current sensitivity of the order of ~10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and threshold voltage selectivity, ∆S<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Vth</inf> = 207 for gelatin (k = 12). The study demonstrates that the proposed biosensor is excellent contender for point-of-care biosensing applications.

title: Free-space Optical Accelerometer with High Dynamic Range Using Phase Generated Carrier Demodulation Achieved via Integrated Coil Modulation
authors: Yuqi Meng, Shivansh Madan, Neal A. Hall
abstract: This work demonstrates a free-space optical displacement sensor based on a Michelson-type interferometer employing the phase generated carrier demodulation technique. The sensor utilizes a vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser (VCSEL) and a proof mass comprising a coil-wound bobbin and a diffraction grating serving as a beam splitter. The bobbin is modulated sinusoidally out-of-band to encode the proof mass displacement resulting from seismic ground motion. Signal demodulation is achieved by extracting in-phase and quadrature components of the proof mass displacement and phase unwrapping, enabling displacement measurement across multiple optical fringes. Experimental results show that the sensor accurately recovers ground motion when excited by single-frequency and broadband test signals, with performance validated against a reference geophone. The presented scheme eliminates the need for active force-feedback control, thereby enabling designs with higher mechanical compliance and higher SNR than would otherwise be achievable. Future work will address comprehensive noise and dynamic range analysis, including the effects of shot noise and laser RIN.

title: Robust Multiplicative Control in Chemical Reaction Networks
authors: Emmanouil Alexis, Clarence W. Rowley, José L. Avalos
abstract: Achieving complex multi-species control objectives is essential for engineering advanced autoregulated biomolecular devices. This paper addresses the problem of robust steady-state tracking for outputs defined as multiplicative combinations of biomolecular species concentrations. We first introduce a control architecture realized via chemical reaction networks that steers the product of two target species concentrations in the controlled network to a prescribed value. A robust stability analysis is provided for closed-loop system families with distinct structural characteristics. The proposed framework is then extended to a more general formulation capable of regulating arbitrary monomial outputs involving multiple species. Numerical simulations of representative examples corroborate the theoretical results and illustrate the effectiveness of our approach.

title: Adaptive Sliding Mode Path Following Control for Autonomous Ground Vehicles with Uncertain Mass and Cornering Stiffness
authors: Hongliang Li, Heng Wang, Gang Liu, Qing Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper presents a novel integrated estimation-control framework to solve the path following problem for autonomous ground vehicles under coupled uncertainties in mass and cornering stiffness. The proposed method is characterized by the synergistic integration of three key innovations: 1) a fuzzy confidence-enhanced recursive least-squares algorithm (FC-RLS-MFF) that employs a rule-based mechanism to dynamically assess excitation sufficiency, ensuring robust online mass estimation; 2) a tightly-coupled identification scheme, where the estimated mass actively refines cornering stiffness in real time through tire vertical load updates, departing from the use of fixed nominal values; and 3) an adaptive fast nonsingular terminal sliding mode controller (AFNTSMC) that leverages this real-time parameter stream to enable model-based compensation, rather than mere disturbance rejection. Co-simulations, including ablation studies, validate the framework and demonstrate that this tight integration yields emergent performance gains, such as a significant reduction in path-tracking error.

title: An Enhanced YOLOv11-Based Algorithm for Identifying Unhelmeted Power Plant Workers
authors: Qinbo Ren, Yapeng Su, Kuilong Zhang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: Power plants represent high-risk industrial environments where head injuries account for $\mathbf{4 2 \%}$ of fatal accidents due to non-compliance with safety helmet regulations. This paper proposes a novel enhanced You Only Look Once version 11 (YOLOv11)-based algorithm (PowerHelmet-YOLO) tailored for real-time detection of unhelmeted workers in power plant scenarios. The algorithm integrates deformable convolutional network v2 (DCNv2) to handle geometric transformations and occlusions, a recalibrated feature pyramid (RFP) for multi-scale feature fusion, and a dual-threshold tracking mechanism to address target occlusion. Experimental validation on the PowerPlant-Safety Dataset (PPSD) demonstrates that PowerHelmet-YOLO achieves a mean average precision $\left(\mathrm{mAP}_{50}\right)$ of $94.2 \%$, precision of $93.7 \%$, and recall of $91.8 \%$, outperforming state-of-the-art models including YOLOv10n and YOLO with Deformable Convolutional Network v2 and Recalibrated Feature Pyramid (YOLO-DCNRFP). The model runs at 326 Frames Per Second (FPS) on edge devices, meeting the real-time monitoring requirements of power plant operations. This research provides a reliable technical solution for automated safety compliance monitoring in complex industrial environments.

title: Classification of Liver Disease Using Random Forest, Decision Tree, and K-Nearest Neighbors
authors: Syarifah Diana, Ghinaa Zain Nabiilah, Nixon, Fathan Aghnia Akmal
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Liver disease is a major global health problem affecting millions of people worldwide. Early detection is crucial for improving prevention, treatment options, and recovery outcomes. This study used a dataset of 1,700 records containing demographic information and laboratory test results, each labeled to indicate the presence or absence of liver disease. The goal of this study was to develop a machine learning model capable of identifying patterns in the data to aid in the early prediction of liver disease. Three models were used in this study. The Random Forest, Decision Tree, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) were evaluated on both training and testing datasets. Among the three, the Random Forest model demonstrated superior performance, achieving the highest accuracy and balanced generalization, making it the most effective method for predicting liver disease in this study. This research highlights the potential of machine learning techniques to support the timely diagnosis and management of liver disease.

title: The Role of Institutional Investment in Enhancing Academic Performance through Smart Education Systems
authors: Meryem Ziz, Sophia Faris, Khalifa Mansouri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Understanding how universities allocate resources to support student success has become increasingly urgent as higher education systems undergo rapid digital transformation. This study analyzes the impact of institutional investments on student academic performance, focusing on six key areas: financial aid, academic support services, infrastructure, educational technologies, extracurricular activities, and health services. The main objective of this research is to understand the complexity of institutional investments and their contribution to the creation of smart, technologically enriched learning environments. A literature review is conducted to emphasize the strategic role of these investments in improving educational experience and student success. In addition, a quantitative study was conducted among students enrolled in Moroccan higher education institutions to empirically assess the effect of these investments on academic performance. Results show that strategic investments, particularly in educational technologies, financial aid, and infrastructure, are the main factors improving academic outcomes and promoting the development of sustainable Smart Education systems. The study contributes to the growing body of research on institutional resource allocation in developing countries with higher education contexts and offers practical guidance to pursue sustainable digital transformation.

title: A Parametric MAXQ Reinforcement Learning Accelerator on FPGA with Outlook Toward Quantum Electronic Architectures
authors: Aziz Oukaira, Ahmed Lakhssassi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study focuses on the development of a parametric MAXQ Q-Learning hardware accelerator implemented on FPGA, within the broader evolution of Reinforcement Learning (RL) toward hybrid classical-quantum architectures. Recent advances in quantum electronics offer promising opportunities for accelerated value-function optimization and improved energy efficiency. In this work, we present a complete VHDL-based hardware implementation deployed on a CycloneV (5CSEMA5F31C6) FPGA platform. The proposed architecture supports Q-value precision ranging from 8 to 128 bits, state sizes $N=8,16,32$, and action sets $Z=4,8,16$. Experimental evaluation was conducted using Quartus Prime under a 2 ns timing constraint. Performance metrics include logic resource utilization (LUTs, LUTRAMs, flip-flops), maximum achievable clock frequency, and post-Place & Route power consumption.

title: Power Quality Analysis in a PV Integrated DVR System Using Optimizing Based Approach
authors: Devi Prasad Acharya, Soumya Ranjan Das, Bhagabat Panda, Ganesh Kudva, Jayalakshimi N. S
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: The production of electrical power depends heavily on renewable energy sources (RES), especially in systems attached to utility. Power quality problems result from RES's communication with the grid. Power quality issues cause voltage sag, swell, and harmonics in the grid, which negatively affect consumers. A reimbursement device, like the Dynamic Voltage Restorer (DVR), is the most economical way to address the aforementioned issues. A PV-incorporated DVR is suggested in this study to address the problems with power quality. The principal goal of this research is to improve the quality of power by designing the PI controller for DVR using the special grasshopper optimization technique, which further increases the controller's robustness and efficiency. The system's dynamic functionality has been tested with the proposed method with addition of a nonlinear load and the voltage and current sag conditions are observed and analyzed. SIMULINK software is used to verify the proposed system's functionality in real time. The findings indicate that the design effectively limits harmonic, degrees of voltage sag and swell at the load side within the IEEE accepted limit.

title: Review of Stress Detection Methods Based on TinyML
authors: Lina Abousalama, Sanae Mazouz
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Stress has become a serious public health issue with significant effects on physical and mental health, highlighting the need for reliable methods for early and continuous detection. Wearable sensors enable non-invasive monitoring of physiological signals related to stress. However, cloud-based solutions face challenges such as latency, energy consumption and privacy restrictions, which limit their suitability for resource-constrained wearable devices. Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) represents an alternative approach by enabling on-device stress detection in low-power embedded systems. This paper provides an overview of TinyML-based stress detection approaches published between 2020 and 2025. It describes the most common physiological signals (photoplethysmography (PPG), electrocardiography (ECG), heart rate variability (HRV), electrodermal activity (EDA), Heart Rate (HR), skin temperature, respiration (RESP) and triaxial accelerometer), as well as commonly used benchmark datasets (Wearable Stress and Affect Detection (WESAD), Nurse Stress Dataset, Montreal Stress Test (MIST), ISRUC and SWELL), and hardware platforms (ESP32, RP2040-based Raspberry Pi Pico, STM32 series microcontrollers, Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense and heterogeneous platforms such as InfiniWolf). A comparative analysis evaluates performance, memory usage, latency and deployment feasibility of different TinyML architectures. The results show that lightweight models, particularly deep neural networks (DNNs) and one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (1D CNNs) combined with optimization techniques such as quantization, allow for an optimal trade-off between accuracy and resource usage. Future research directions and key challenges for developing scalable and reliable TinyML-based stress detection systems for wearable healthcare applications are also discussed.

title: IMU-Based Feature Engineering for Early Detection of UAV Motor Faults: A Study on RflyMAD
authors: Abdelilah Zadid, Soufiane Hamida, Amal Tmiri, Oumaima Majdoubi, Bouchaib Cherradi, Maryam Domar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Motor-fault early warning for multirotor UAVs often relies on propulsion telemetry (RPM/current/ESC) or dedicated vibration sensors, increasing integration complexity and SWaP and limiting applicability on cost-constrained platforms. We study a stricter setting: predicting motor faults 3 s ahead using IMU-only sensing (3-axis accelerometer and gyroscope), without propulsion measurements or control signals. Experiments on the RflyMAD Software-in-the-Loop benchmark follow a strict anti-leakage protocol with flightdisjoint train/test splitting, causal past-only features, and operating thresholds learned from training out-of-fold predictions and frozen before test evaluation. We compare a RAW-IMU baseline using instantaneous samples with a causal multi-scale feature-engineering pipeline $(0.5 / 1 / 3 \mathrm{~s}$ windows with lag $g=0.5 \mathrm{~s}$) extracting temporal statistics, derivative descriptors, and spectral features. Despite ROC-AUC $\approx \mathbf{0. 9 0}$, RAW-IMU achieves only PR-AUC $\approx 0.36-0.38$ and Recall $\approx$ 0.27-0.29 at $\mathrm{FPR} \approx 2 \%$ on unseen flights. In contrast, engineered features reach PR-AUC up to 0.85 and Recall $\approx 0.77$ at comparable FPR under frozen thresholds. Feature-importance analysis shows multi-second and frequency-related descriptors dominate, suggesting precursors are encoded in evolving inertial dynamics rather than instantaneous amplitudes. These results support IMU-only early warning as a viable low-SWaP option when robust causal temporal descriptors are used.
